- Relocation-the woes, the grows and glows | Lisl Foss | TEDxALC: Kind: captions Language: en Have any of you cried yourselves to sleep after moving to a new place? No? Well, you would be in good company here today. Because my big question is why is it so disruptive to close off things in one place and start a new life in another? Even if this change was something we desired and actively pursued. Searching for the answer to this question led me to my experience of relocating to Mauritius almost a year ago. I had left behind a 30-year life of family, friends, and a busy career to start a job in a country I had never even visited before. Eight months into my new life the honeymoon phase was over. What had initially been an exciting challenge had just turned into routine. And the novelty of my surroundings was no longer sustaining me. I was feeling tired, depressed, demotivated, and mostly confused about what was happening to me because I had wanted this move. I realized that if this change was having such a big impact on me it was likely that there might be others who could relate to what I was going through and who might currently even be experiencing some of these adjustment challenges. In doing the research for my presentation, I was surprised to find that there are in fact predictable patterns to the woes, grows, and glows of relocation. And that it's likely that most of us will go through them when we move to a new place. So, today I will be taking a look at what we may expect when we move to a new place and also consider some ways in which we might deal with the impact of this on our lives. Why is relocation to a new place so stressful? One of the reasons is that at core we are creatures of habit and we like the safe and the known. Moving strips us of the familiar trappings of our life. We may become completely disorientated and sometimes feel completely overwhelmed by the sheer newness of everything. As we try to adjust, we may long for the predictability and the sense of control that we left behind. And we may even start doubting the wisdom of our decision to relocate. First night I spent in Mauritius was possibly the worst of my life. I hardly slept, waking every couple of hours with a feeling of utter dread in my stomach. What on earth had I done? Giving up a successful and a happy life to settle on an island in the middle of nowhere where I knew no one. But when we are feeling stretched, alone, and scared in a new place, it's just about impossible to connect with that craving for adventure and independence that brought us here in the first place. These early days of relocation can also be a time of loneliness and fear. We are worried that we won't be able to cope with our work. We worry about making new friends and that the life we left behind won't be the same when we return. That family and friends will have forgotten about us. And there's also sometimes the pressure to give something back to those at home because of the opportunity granted us, because we were the chosen ones above others. The first time I went back home, I didn't know what to expect. How would I feel when I met up with my old friends? Would we still have something left to say to one another? Would they want to hear about my new life? Would I still even care about theirs? During these early days, a sense of isolation and emotional numbness may permeate our days leaving us feeling confused and disconnected. The only solace may well be contact with family and friends from our old lives giving us the strength to carry on. When I arrived in Mauritius, my colleagues and I were accommodated in Elysée Residence. And in those days the Wi-Fi there was very unreliable. But I happened to discover that the bathrooms in the sky room for some reason had the very best Wi-Fi in the building. during my first month in Mauritius, I spent many hours sitting on a closed toilet talking with the people back home, with them wondering why on earth my voice had such a strange echoy ring to it. Leaving home may sometimes bring up memories of unresolved losses from the past. This can make adjusting to the new place even harder. It can feel like there's a kind of noise in our heads which makes meeting obligations and commitments in our new context seem surprisingly diff- difficult, sometimes even insurmountable. We may feel vulnerable and helpless as if hit by a self-imposed earthquake wondering what is happening to us. And we may fear that we will never get our happy, confident selves back again. All these unsettling experiences can be thought of as anxiety stress, a common outcome of relocation. It is an overwhelmed, uneasy, apprehensive, and worrisome state of mind which makes us feel inadequate and unable to cope. It's interesting to note that monkeys who were subjected to the stress of relocation in a research study suffered just as we do exhibiting increases in neurotic behavior, elevated levels of cortisol, which is the stress hormone, and sleep disturbances. So, it is not a figment of our imagination that moving is demanding and exhausting. And it will probably not work to simply pull ourselves together and get over it. Fortunately, there is light at the end of the tunnel. And if we embrace or at most survive the challenges of relocation it can launch us onto an exciting path of expansion. There are ways in which moving to a new place can strengthen us, increase our resilience, and make us more resourceful than ever before. The grows of relocation. One such positive outcome is that in order to deal with the anxiety and helplessness of being out of our comfort zone, we become more flexible. We develop new skills and resort to different approaches even though these novel behaviors might be uncomfortable or scary at first. The level of discomfort associated with my own growth process took me completely by surprise. Before moving to Mauritius, I had longed for a new challenge that would force me to know myself better and develop new skills. I couldn't believe my luck when I was actually offered my current job. To grow seemed such a noble and amazing thing to be able to do. But I never dreamt it would be so difficult and in such unexpected ways. The lessons I have learned have terrified me, challenged me, and almost defeated me. But ultimately, they pushed me to grow and I feel different on the inside now. As with all major transitions, moving also provides a powerful opportunity to heal parts of ourselves that don't emerge unless we are in the midst of significant change. In my own case, living alone away from my family initially made me quite anxious. I hated that feeling that I could fall over and die in my apartment and no one would even know. Eventually, out of desperation, I trained myself to look in the mirror and say, "But you are not alone. Look, there you are. There is somebody here with you. No, I'm learning to enjoy my own company and sometimes even relish the peace and quiet of my apartment rather than feeling afraid of the isolation or wondering where all the other people are having fun without me. Relocation offers further occasion for growth by teaching us to be more independent emotionally, physically, and financially. It increases our resilience and as we find ways to thrive in the new place, we feel more confident of our ability to cope. We understand what is important to us and what we need to make our lives work. The emotional decluttering that happens when we no longer define ourselves in terms of our previous lives creates internal space which allows for new qualities to emerge. We are already rebuilding ourselves as part of the adjustment process. So it becomes easier to update our ways of learning and working to be more efficient or relevant. My previous position was in a traditional 100-year-old university and I often felt pressure to limit myself to conform to the status quo. But upon starting my new job, I was experiencing change on so many levels that it seemed a relatively simple thing to also adjust the way I work. This has been a liberating and exciting process and I have grown professionally as a result. So there definitely are ways in which moving can help us to become stronger. And the evidence suggests that after we have grown and stretched and adjusted to our changed circumstances we can expect some happy times and start experiencing what I call the glows of relocation. One of these is in the area of relationships. Strangers who start a life together in a different place often form lifelong friendships because of their shared vulnerability, especially if the going is tough. Being a newcomer in a community also offers the potential for new friendships and new professional connections. We also learn to reach out more. Away from our usual support networks, we are more likely to ask for help and become more open-minded about accepting invitations from new friends. All of which makes our lives richer and fuller. Furthermore our increased need for support during relocation may lead to a deepening of existing I can honestly say that I would not have survived the first 6 months in Mauritius without long and emotional WhatsApp conversations with family and friends in other parts of the world. They provided a space where I no longer had to hold it all together where I gave myself permission to feel and to be comforted. When a friend from my past life understood what I was going through it cemented the bonds between us. And these relationships are now an even greater source of pleasure in my life. Sometimes the boldness of my move to Mauritius has inspired others back home to make changes in their own lives. Being a role model in this way makes all the discomfort and stress seem worthwhile and it really helps me to trust myself and my decisions going forward. A further glow of the relocation process relates to the way it forces us to experience life moment by moment. Moving is such an all-consuming phenomenon that it leaves little energy or time for anything but the next job that needs to be done. The big questions about where our lives will go after this phase, whether we will succeed or fail, remain unanswered. I am myself currently learning to move away from my personal unknowns like when will my husband join me in Mauritius? What will happen if he doesn't? What will happen if he does? What will happen to me in my old age? How will I stay financially secure? Will I ever settle properly in Mauritius? And what on earth would I do if I went back home? Instead I am learning to remain in the present experiencing the richness of staying focused on what is in front of me and trusting that all will be well. And finally, another wonderful outcome of relocation is the fresh start that it gives us. To be surrounded by new things and different people is interesting and stimulating. The internal energy generated provides momentum for personal change and we have the opportunity to rebuild our lives according to our own needs and goals away from the expectations and constraints of our previous lives. Talking about constraints from previous lives I love swimming and I've often dreamt about being one of those old ladies who go swimming every morning regardless of the weather. But I also happen to hate cold water which was always a big problem back home. Fortunately, here in Mauritius, the wonderful temperature of the sea makes it possible for me to be that crazy lady. And my daily swims have become a new source of joy in my life. So this story will hopefully have a happy ending. Nevertheless the stress associated with moving can do damage to our health and well-being. Relocation is commonly rated as life's third most taxing event following death and divorce. So I would like to end by sharing with you some best practices towards consciously managing the process so that the discomfort of relocation can also bring you equivalent levels of growth and healing. Number one. Remember every day how courageous you are. Cultivate a sense of adventure and explore your new surroundings. Take the risk of getting involved. But also have a safe place to retreat to when you've had enough. Secondly be gentle with yourself and don't be afraid of strong emotions. They are quite normal and will change as you settle. Be patient and accept help and maintain those important relationships back home. Never compare your current life with things back home. Rather focus on the present. Find small things to be grateful for and set a time every day to do something you love or spending time in a beautiful place. Cultivate a kind and positive attitude towards everyone around you, but don't compromise to fit in. Being yourself will more quickly attract like-minded people to you and you will make friends more easily. And finally gently and fully grieve the loss of the life you once lived. Unless we let ourselves feel the sadness of things left behind and let them go, it can be difficult to adapt to the new environment. This takes time and it must happen at our own pace. It is not a race. Ladies and gentlemen, relocation is disruptive for all the reasons discussed here But it is disruptive also in the very best sense of the word. The sad, lonely, and distressed phase usually paves the way for better things to come. In short relocation can feel like one of the worst times of our lives but it may also launch us to the very best. Thank you.

- 3 Ways to Better Connect with Your Coworkers | The Way We Work, a TED series: Kind: captions Language: en People feel pretty disconnected at work right now: disconnected from their organizations, from their jobs, and especially from their colleagues. You might feel too burned out to do anything about this last one or maybe think that it doesn't matter. But actually, our relationships with our coworkers play a huge role in determining how successful, innovative and happy we'll be at work. [The Way We Work] Our work relationships give us two valuable things: social support and access to information. And research shows it's the combination that can make us happier and more successful. I'm a social scientist and I study how the way we collaborate affects our well-being and professional success. I have a few tips, and you don't have to be a social butterfly to give them a try. OK, tip number one. Build trust by opening up. Having a close friend at work makes us more effective at our jobs and definitely improves our well-being. If you get along with your coworkers, you probably already have a sense of this, but our work friends are important for another reason and it has to do with access to information. People don't just share their best ideas with anyone. We share with people we know and trust. I ran a multi-year study at Google to find out why some teams are successful and others fail. And what we found is that the teams who trust each other do so much better because people on those teams feel safe sharing their best ideas. So how can you start to build that trust? There's a concept called a vulnerability loop, and the basic idea is that when you’re vulnerable in front of somebody, it makes it a lot easier for them to be vulnerable in front of you. We usually think you have to trust somebody before you can open up. But what this research suggests is that often, trust comes after we've been vulnerable and people have responded empathetically. So taking that risk to share that this week you feel a little off because your kid's starting at a new school or maybe you’re really worried about the status of your big project, that can actually go a long way towards building a sense of trust that can make you and your teams much more effective. OK, tip number two, be a joiner. Surprisingly, our closest friends aren't the best people for giving us access to new information. It's often our acquaintances because they connect us to different social circles and consequently new ways of thinking. Social scientists call these relationships weak ties, and when it comes to innovation, they're more important than our strongest connections. For example, in one study, I found that people who had many weak ties that connected different groups in a large organization were more likely to have high performance scores, more likely to be promoted and even more likely to say they were a part of a creative breakthrough. The best way I've seen for developing weak ties is to join groups outside of your day-to-day work. Things like philanthropic organizations, sports clubs or employee resource groups are great examples. You never know. Tobin from Legal, who you met playing pickleball, might have a great idea that can really help you out in your next project. And for leaders trying to spark innovation, having a regular trivia group might be more effective the best-run strategy summit. OK, this last tip is especially helpful for managers. Tip three: Make it a little bit easier for people to belong to something. A number of years ago, I helped redesign the onboarding process at a big company. At the time, the onboarding week was maybe informative, but it didn't do a lot to create meaningful connection. So I ran an experiment. Every week, I assigned new employees to one of five groups, each with a different colored T-shirt, and something amazing started to happen. Almost immediately, people wearing yellow T-shirts started having breakfast together. People in the green T-shirts started walking between the different sessions together. And at the end of the week, we blew by our happy hour budget because people hung out together so much longer than they ever had. The best part is, after that onboarding week, many of those people stayed in touch for years, which became a great source of weak ties across the organization. The reason the T-shirt experiment worked so well is because it gave people an easy way to identify the group they belong to. You could be creative here. Even things like swag, T-shirts and project code names can go a long way towards creating a sense of belonging. Look, people feel really isolated and disconnected right now. That's not surprising, we've all just been through a global pandemic. The best part about reconnecting is that it doesn't just benefit you, it benefits the whole community that you help create.

- 7 Stages of a Long Distance Relationship: Kind: captions Language: en - [Amanda] Hey there Psych2Go family. We at Psych2Go are grateful and humbled by your love and support. Here at Psych2Go our mission is to make content on mental health and self-help more accessible to everyone. So, let's continue to navigate everyday psychology. (acoustic music) Research shows an estimated 75% of college students will be in a long-distance relationship at some point during university. Even though social media, messaging apps and video calls make it easy for couples to stay connected while apart for extended periods of time, long-distance relationships are anything but easy. Studies have shown higher break up rates in long-distance relationships that in close distance ones. Oh, dear! And while some long-distance relationships even start out that way when couples meet online, others transition from close distance to long-distance relationships. Here are seven stages in a long-distance relationship. One, decision. The first chapter in the book of long-distance relationships is the decision to try to make long-distance work. When one partner lives far away or is faced with an opportunity or obligation that requires travel, and the other partner cannot or chooses not to go with them, the couple has to choose between breaking up or entering into a Common factors that cause couples to consider a long distance relationship are travel due to job description or promotions, a partner in the military, education and emergency or family members moving. During the decision stage, partners evaluate whether they wanna commit to a long-distance relationship and others decided it's best to simply end their relationship. Two parting. Once both partners have agreed to pursue a long-distance relationship, so begins the second chapter titled parting. They'll try to spend every minute together before they're separated to make up for the weeks, months or years they'll spend apart. This is also the stage where partner should set boundaries and make rules to keep the relationship healthy when it transitions to long-distance. Three, transition. The transition stage comes after one or both partners have left, launching the long-distance part of their relationship. In this stage, partners might be in a state of denial or simply too busy with the practicals of moving and adjusting to life without their partner to dwell on how the relationship has changed. They'll probably message or call their partner frequently and keep them updated on every little thing, they might still feel like they're still in a close distance relationship. And then comes four, realization. Were you just about to call them to meet you for coffee? Has the fact that you can't meet up with them whenever you like just kinda sunk in? Do you feel at a loss because you could really use a hug from them right now? If so, you've now entered the next chapter, realization. During this stage, couples begin to process what life is like without their partner. They must face the reality that they can't depend on their partner to be there for them physically anymore. The realization stage, while painful also teaches partners to be more independent, have better time management and improve their communication. Five, jealousy. Did they just post a photo of them having fun at the beach with new friends? Are they planning to go to an event with their new colleagues? Like, really? Here you are not being able to even eat properly because you miss them so much while they've already made new friends. Jealousy is a common stage in long-distance relationships. In a study 125 U.S. college students who had been or were currently in long-distance relationships, concluded that jealousy was the most commonly experienced negative emotion while they were apart. Participants in the study reported feeling jealous, not only of potential romantic interest, but also a friends and family who got to spend more time with their significant other than they did. Six, doubt. Now comes the climax. Relational uncertainty is another typical stage in long-distance relationships. Long-distance partners reported that the longer they went without seeing their partner face to face the greater their relationship uncertainty became. And if one or both partners have doubts about the relationship before the transition to long-distance, their relationship has a weaker chance of surviving. And seven, validation. Validation is the last chapter in the book. Partners in a successful long distance relationship will find themselves returning to the stage again and again. While the unsuccessful ones might end in stage six, doubt, study show that the long-distance partners are just as satisfied with their relationships as close distance partners when their partner actively listen to them and responded in positive ways. This means although long-distance partners were not face to face they still felt like their relationship improved after phone or online communication, as long as they could tell their partner supported them. This study points to the importance of disclosure and open and honest conversations to build trust and validation. Long-distance relationships are high maintenance, but very rewarding. Once partners decide to commit to a long-distance relationship, they'll inevitably go through new experiences without their partner. may come with jealousy and doubts, but they also offer an opportunity for partners to step back and re-evaluate their relationship. And if they find themselves choosing each other again and again, then their relationship has a strong chance of working out. Are you in a long-distance relationship now? Have you observed these stages in such relationships around you? Do let us know in the comments below. Also remember to like and share this video with those who are or in the near future might be in a long-distance relationship. Don't forget to subscribe to Psych2Go and hit the notification icon for more videos. Thanks for watching. (acoustic music continues)

So, I definitely think of terms of like months whenever I want to be somewhere. this because it's just cheaper overall. - Married But Living in Different States — Does USCIS Approve That?: Kind: captions Language: en We want to get married, but live in different states. Hi, I'm Jim Hacking, immigration lawyer practicing law throughout the United States. out of our offices in St. Louis, San Diego, Washington DC, and Chicago, Illinois. In this video, we're talking about situations where someone like on an F visa or a work visa, uh, marries a US citizen or lawful permanent resident and wants to apply for adjustment of status based on that marriage. The plan is, to eventually live together, but in the short term, we' don't know exactly when that's going to happen. So this is a scenario that comes up often. We talk to people all the time here on international student visas like Fs or Js, and they have fallen in love with a US citizen. They go regularly to see their partner, or maybe their partner had to move right before they got married or right before they applied for an immigration benefit. Maybe they went to school together and they both graduated, and one got a job in Cleveland, one got a job on OPT in, uh, Cincinnati. And so we have them in two different cities. How are we gonna handle that? How can this case be approved? Well, whenever we come across a scenario like this, I always, uh, ask the people that are involved in the situation, the spouses or the fiances, and I say, "Well, three things. Number one, do you have a legitimate reason for living apart? Like can we document the reason why?" So if you have a job in Cincinnati for Procter & Gamble and you're making twice as much as you could make if you were living with your spouse in Cleveland, that's a good reason to be separated from each other. And so they're going to want to look at that. They're going to want to see proof of that. You're going to have to walk them through why you actually live together. Second thing is, what are you doing to see each other? Do you go see each other once every two months like the guy who called me on the Immigration Answer Show the other day? Or do you go down every weekend or every other weekend or go down three days a week? Whatever it is, do you, do you spend time together? Do you appear to be in love? Do you appear to be people who want to spend as much time as humanly possible that are sort of sad when you have to go back to Cleveland or go back to Cincinnati? Uh, whatever the case may be. And so that's number two, which is: what are you doing now to spend time together? So really what I'm talking about is past, present, and future. Past is: what's the real reason why you have to live apart? Present is: wha- what are you doing now to stay together as much as you can? And then the future, what's the long-term plan? And that's the third question. What's the long-term plan for staying together and being together? And so if you have, uh, one more year of school to do and your spouse had to move away to start their kick-ass job at Procter & Gamble, that's going to make a lot more sense than if you have an open, no decision, no time anywhere in the near future. The officer wants to believe you. The officer wants to understand, "Yeah, this makes sense. This couple is living apart just temporarily because one of them got this great job," or something like that. It's still gonna be a high hurdle. It's still gonna be a high burden. You're still gonna have to satisfy them that the marriage. is real. This is especially true if the beneficiary was out of status. So when a beneficiary is out of status and marries someone and they don't live with them, not good, not good. You're already starting behind because they think that' you were out of status, so you paid someone to get married so that you could then get in status, that your marriage is inherently not real. It's just to get an immigration benefit. When you don't live together, that's just giving them more ammunition to hit you over the head with, and I've even had people who were out of status, weren't living together, and didn't have a plan on when to live together, and I frankly turned that case down because it was gonna be very, very hard to approve. So those are the things that you need to think about. I think this is especially true in 2025 where they're looking for reasons to say no. So you're going to have to show that the relationship is real. I would say that, you know, I always ask people, "On a scale of one to ten, how much evidence do you have? One being very little, ten. being a lot." I would say that if you're living apart, you need 12. You need extra evidence. You need longer detail. You know, what I don't like is when people live apart and they haven't really known each other all that long. That just seems screwy. I think USCIS says, "Hmm. I think this is just to get Abdullah or, uh, Vinesh a green card, not really because Abdullah and Vinesh are in love with somebody else." So that's the deal with that. - Living In Different States? Marriage Green Card Adjustment of Status: Kind: captions Language: en In today's video, we're talking about spouses living in two different states during your marriage adjustment or status case, some tips, and how to best present your case. Hi guys, my name is Cassenia. I make videos for people who are going through the familybased green card process. I'm not an attorney. My videos are based on public information, my own experience and the experience of my subscribers. And the purpose of my channel is to give you guys the confidence to go through this process completely on your own. So, like I mentioned in the beginning of this video, this is for those of you who are going through the marital adjustment of status case, but you and your spouse happen to live in different states. While it is not a complete disqualifier of your marriage green card case, spouses living in different states may have to go through additional scrutiny. So, one of the things that will most definitely happen in your case if you and your spouse live in different states is that you will be most likely invited to a green card interview. Of course, for the most part in 2025, we're seeing an increase in marriage green card interviews in general. But I do want to tell you that even in the past few years when a lot of marital interviews were waved, the spouses who lived apart in different states for the most part were still invited for an interview. USCIS officers firstly wanted to see if both spouses will show up to the interview in the first place and also they usually would want to clarify during the interview why you live apart. Now, to get ahead of this issue, one of the things you can do is to actually present a testimony or a signed statement explaining why you live apart to make sure that a USCIS officer understands that this is just a temporary arrangement and for what reason and how do you plan to establish your future life together? how soon it will actually happen and what steps are you currently taking to maintain your marital relationship. Which brings me to my second point. You want to supplement your case with lots of additional bonafide marital evidence. And I'm not only talking about photographs or trips, booking confirmations. Those are all great pieces of evidence, but they're not as strong as financial and legally binding pieces of evidence. For example, filing your taxes together as married filing jointly or having each other on health insurance, life insurance, car insurance, etc. Also, make an effort to visit your spouse as much as possible. USCIS wants to make sure that you do make an effort to go see your spouse, especially during important dates like holidays or dates that are related to your marriage and your relationship in general. And finally, I do get this question a lot. Spouses who live in different locations, what address do we use on the forms and where do we send our cases? On your forms, you should be writing your physical address exactly as it is now. So, if you and your spouse are located in two different states, you should be writing exactly in those states where you're located. But for the mailing address portion, I do recommend that you choose one of the spouse's addresses to use as a mailing address for all USCIS correspondents and even for your evidence. Now, where to send your file for spouses who are eligible for concurrent filing? So, like a spouse of a US citizen who is eligible to file I130 and I485 at the same time. If you live in different states, it is likely that the address where you must send your I130 petition will be different to the address where you must send your I485 application because the I130 petition belongs to the petitioner. So to the logbox facility that is associated with the address of the petitioner and I485 and all the rest of the forms will generally be mailed to the logbox facility that is associated with the beneficiary's address. So, in that case, to avoid confusion and avoid splitting up all the forms, send the I130 petition online through the petitioner's USCIS account. And the rest of the forms, such as the I485, the affidavit of support, the medical exam, the employment and travel authorizations, those forms can then be sent by mail to the lockbox facility associated with the beneficiary's address. Of course, don't forget to include the copy of the I130 online receipt notice in your packet to make sure that USCIS can locate and connect the two cases. Additionally, in the I130 petition, in part of the application that asks where the beneficiary will apply for adjustment of status, you would want to indicate the field office that is closely located to the beneficiary's current residence. But do keep in mind that you will have to travel to that location when your interview will be scheduled. And a final bonus tip for spouses who are in the military. Your spouse may not be able to attend the marriage green card interview because they are deployed or stationed somewhere else and are unable to leave their location. USCIS does have a policy for these situations. Your best bet is to keep your interview date and simply attend it alone. But do provide proof of your military spouse's ID and their current assignment. So, I hope that you found this video useful. I hope that you are able to navigate this process freely without any difficulties. If you haven't done so yet, please subscribe to my channel, give this video a thumbs up, and I hope to see you guys in my next videos.

- If We Live In Different States, Where Do We File For Divorce Based On Residency?: Kind: captions Language: en If we live in different states, where do we file for divorce based on residency? Are you and your spouse living in different states and wondering where to file for divorce? This is a common question that many couples face. Let's break down the residency requirements for filing for divorce when spouses are in different states. When it comes to divorce, each state has its own residency requirements. These rules determine how long a spouse must have lived in a state before they can file for divorce there. Generally, the divorce must be filed in the state where at least one spouse meets that state's residency requirements. Most states requiring a residency period that typically ranges from about 6 weeks to one year. For example, in Nevada, you only need to live there for 6 weeks before filing. On the other hand, New York has a longer requirement, needing up to 1 year of residency. It is important to note that only one spouse needs to meet the residency requirement for the divorce to be filed in that state. This means that if either spouse has lived in a state long enough according to that state's law, the divorce can be filed there. Some states have additional rules. For instance, Texas requires that a spouse must have lived in the state for 6 months and also in the specific county for at least 90 days before filing. This means that if you are considering filing for divorce in Texas, you need to ensure you meet both of these residency requirements. These residency rules are in place to ensure that the court has proper authority to hear the case. They also help prevent what is called forum shopping. This is when a spouse tries to file in a state that has laws more favorable to them without having a real connection to that state. So if you and your spouse live in different states, the spouse who meets the residency requirement in their state can file for divorce there. If neither spouse meets the residency requirement in the other state, then filing cannot occur until those requirements are satisfied. In practical terms, the timing and location of filing depend on where either spouse currently resides and how long they have lived there. Understanding these residency rules is essential for determining the correct jurisdiction for your divorce. This ensures that the divorce is legally valid and recognized by the courts. In summary, remember that divorce is filed in the state where at least one spouse meets that state's residency requirement. Residency periods vary by state, typically between 6 weeks and one year. Only one spouse needs to meet the residency requirement to file and some states may requiring additional county residency. Knowing these details can help you navigate the divorce process more smoothly.

  8. @beaubreau: Every functional system is built on exchange. Value is created, transferred, and returned in some form. As systems scale, the likelihood of abuse increases; no large system remains fair on trust alone. That is why regulation exists, not to suppress exchange, but to prevent exploitation where incentives break down.

Capitalism is not a self sustaining force. It requires structure to function. Private ownership and capital accumulation generate wealth, but taxation is the mechanism that allows that wealth to be reinvested into shared infrastructure, stability, and enforcement of the rules that keep markets operating. Without regulation and taxation, markets concentrate power, distort competition, and eventually undermine themselves.

Taxes are not separate from capitalism; they are a product of it. Capital must exist before it can be taxed, and taxation only has meaning in a system where private capital is generated. Remove either side of that relationship and the system collapses. 

Used to we would exchange livestock or fabric. Both require work.  You could say there were far more sole proprietors back in the day when we owned our own businesses, grew our own food and exchanged those goods for things we needed. It doesn't get any more capitalistic than that. 

When many people think about capitalism, they focus almost entirely on exploitation. That reaction is understandable, especially given how often exploitation occurs within capitalist systems. But exploitation is not the defining feature of capitalism; it is a failure of constraints, not the mechanism itself. I say this as someone whose friends largely hold that critical view, so I understand where the instinct comes from.

At its core, capitalism is simply the voluntary exchange of goods, labor, or resources under private ownership. That kind of exchange existed long before we had a word for it. Anytime people traded skills, pooled resources, or agreed that effort would be rewarded with ownership or value, the basic structure was already there. Capitalism is not inherently about domination; it is about coordination.

Interestingly, many systems people see as alternatives to capitalism actually rely on the same underlying principles. Communes, cooperatives, and co ops still require ownership structures, agreed upon rules, and a way to allocate resources and responsibility. Shares may not always be framed as stock, but they function the same way. Contribution creates stake; stake creates accountability.

In fact, I plan to start a commune myself with a group of people. Structurally, it looks much closer to a cooperative business than to an anti capitalist experiment. The land and buildings are collectively owned through an LLC, ownership is defined through buy in and shares, and decisions are made by those who have invested labor or capital. That is not a rejection of capitalism; it is a specific implementation of it.

The real distinction is not capitalism versus community. It is whether a system acknowledges incentives, ownership, and human behavior, or pretends those forces can be ignored without consequence.

  20. @alfars07: حقل الأبحاث الأنثروبولوجية يذكر أن Ethnographic Atlas (أطلس إثنوغرافي لقام بدراسة 1231 مجتمعاً بشرياً) قد صنّف — حسب بياناته من القرن الماضي — أن 4 فقط من هذه المجتمعات مارست نظام Polyandry (أي امرأة لها أكثر من زوج). 

🔍 من هم — أو على الأقل "أشهر" مجتمعات/شعوب — المرتبطون بـ polyandry

رغم أن الأسماء قد تختلف بحسب المصدر، لكن هناك عدد من المجتمعات أو القبائل التي غالباً تُذكر عند الحديث عن polyandry، ومنها:

Tibetans — التبتيون في التبت (أو المناطق الجبلية المجاورة، جبال الهيمالايا) معروف عنهم ممارسة “polyandry” (خاصة “fraternal polyandry” أي أشقاء يتشاركون زوجة). 

Toda — قبيلة في جنوب الهند (Nilgiri Hills) اعتُبرت من الأمثلة الكلاسيكية لـ polyandry. 

Nayar (بعض المراجع تشير إلى بعض أقسام من النّيار في جنوب/غرب الهند) — يُشار أحياناً إلى أنهم مارسوا أنماطاً غير تقليدية من الزواج، بينهما ما قد يُصنف كـ polyandry (أو على الأقل علاقة زوجية متعددة الرجال). 

شعوب/جماعات في جزر بولينيزيا / جنوب المحيط الهادئ — بعض الإشارات في المراجع القديمة تقول إن “جزر المارشال / جزر ماركيساس” (Marquesas Islands) كانت من المناطق التي شوهد فيها polyandry في فترات سابقة. 

⚠️ ما يجب أن تنتبه إليه عند ذكر “4 مجتمعات فقط”

التعداد “4 مجتمعات” من أطلس الإثنوغرافي تعني “مجتمعات موثَّقة في ذلك المسح الكلاسيكي” يتبع معايير صارمة. لكن الأبحاث الحديثة أو المصادر الأنثروبولوجية المتخصصة تشير إلى أن عدد المجتمعات — رسميًا أو غير رسمي — التي مارست أو لا تزال تمارس polyandry ربما أكثر من ذلك. 

“المجتمع” هنا قد يُفهم بمعنى واسع: طائفة، قبيلة، مجموعة إثنية، أو حتى قرية — وليس دولة أو مجموعة كبيرة موحدة.

في كثير من الحالات: polyandry لم يعد يُمارس اليوم كما كان، أو تغيّرت العادات بسبب التحولات الاجتماعية، القوانين، الاقتصاد، أو الهجرة.

إذا تحب — أقدر أحضر لك قائمة مطوّلة (≈ 10–20 مجتمع/قبيلة) حسب دراسات أنثروبولوجية حديثة (من 1900 حتى الآن) — تشمل اسم المجتمع وبلد/منطقة و ما إذا كانوا ما زالوا يمارسون polyandry أو توقفوا. هذا يعطي صورة أوضح من “4 فقط”. تحب أبدأ إعدادها لك الآن؟

- A day in the life of a Dual Pediatrics Resident at Nationwide Children's Hospital: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] The first thing that we do is hand over the patients from the night team to the day team. The entire team comes together, the interns and the residents and the med students and um participate in discussing each patient, any events that happened overnight and updating the day team. Rounds begin after we are done um kind of updating and preparing for the morning. Typically the med student or the intern does a presentation on the patient. If it's a new patient and they're new to the team, they'll go through their their history and what brought them in. Um if it's a patient we already are familiar with and it's not our first day rounding on them. They'll give us updates on um any events that happened overnight and any changes in their clinical status. Uh then we discuss kind of the plan for the day and we put in the orders while we're on rounds and then we move forward. In the inpatient setting, the residents see a wide variety of chronic care kids and high acuity patients uh that require frequently multiple visits and we get to know those patients pretty well. We also see a wide variety of general pediatrics and generally healthy kids that are in for kind of short-term hospitalization. So, it's a nice variety. Typically a second or a thirdyear resident presents a case and then the med students, the interns and the residents all proceed um and participate in kind of asking questions and investigating the case and then we reach kind of a discussion and a conclusion. We try to vary the topics uh from general pediatrics content and curriculum that they need to learn and pertinent information and pertinent things that they need to know at their level particularly related to the rounds that we're or the team service that we're on. We have lectures that are resident driven. We have lectures that are you typically attending driven and then we get together and do have opportunity to practice OMT. The clinic takes place at an offsite center and the patients are generally um pretty healthy kids uh and we do a lot of their primary care and there's a big focus towards continuity of care. Our day concludes with the handing off of patients to the night team and we transition care and update them on plan changes and events that happen throughout the day.

- Living In One State While Working In Another State - Will You Be Double-Taxed?!: Kind: captions Language: en What's going on guys? Alex here and today I'm going to talk about a question that I commonly get about living in one state and working in another. And that question is will I be double taxed on the income that I earn at the state level? It's a great question and the answer is for the most part no you will not be double taxed in many cases but there are some cases where that can be applicable. So let's back up a little bit. Let's try to understand what we're talking about and go from there. So, let's say you live in the sunshine state. You live in California, right? But your employer who pays you through a W2 has you working out of New York, right? So, you reside in California, but for temporary purposes, just for work rellated trips, you go to New York and you earn income in New York. Now, California wants to tax your income because you're a resident. California taxes worldwide income. So they're saying all that income, we're taxing it. But at the same time, since you're working in New York, New York is also saying, "Hey, you're working in New York, we're going to tax that income." And the question then becomes, well, what happens, right? So California's taxing it, New York is taxing it. How do you reconcile all this goodness? So I put together a sample scenario to let you see the mechanics of how all of this can play out. Now, let's take a look at this example, and you'll see in a second tax software. So, what we did in this situation is we basically put together a return for a taxpayer who lives in California, who resides in California, but they work in New York for temporary purposes throughout the year. And I'm going to show you both the New York return and the California return. Get an idea of how this is all playing out. So, for purposes of the income, quite simply, we're looking at $100,000 that this person earned. Let's say they spent the year in New York, uh, going kind of back and forth, but this $100,000 was earned during those times in New York. Now, we want to be careful that we don't establish residency in New York because if it is a situation that you're spending the majority of your time there and essentially New York is your kind of central hub, there could be a residency issue there, which which is a whole separate concept. But in this case, let's just assume that your residence is indeed California and you're a non-resident of New York for the entire year. That doesn't mean you didn't stay there, but that just means you didn't live in New York throughout the year. So with that said, this 100,000 is all applicable to New York. We have the withholdings also applicable at the state level. So let's say there was $5,458 withheld for New York purposes by the state of New York. And that's how this scenario is going to play out. So let's say you come back to California, it's time to file your tax returns. How's this all going to come together? Let's take a look. All right. So on the form 1040, we see that there's 100,000 in income. Nothing too crazy there. That's what we discussed. We're kind of keeping this simple. We're eliminating all the other factors in the scenario, just focusing on this particular point. Let's look at the New York tax return. So, here we have a non-resident party year resident income tax return. In this case, we're assuming non-resident since you did not live there throughout the year. And looking through this return here, we can see that there's some allocation of the income. It's all allocated to New York. So, we don't really have to worry about that. In this scenario, we're assuming that 100,000 was all earned there in the state of New York. And as we look through the return here, we see total New York state taxes of 54.58. And we're done. We were taxed in New York. We paid that tax. What happens on the California return? Let's take a look. Now, going to the California form 540. We take a look here. We see the 100,000 in income is also represented because California taxes worldwide income. Since you're a resident of California, they take that $100,000 into account as well. And kind of working through the return here, we see that the tax calculated is $6,50. There's the exemption credit of 122 that California gives. So that's fine. So we subtract that out. We get 59.28 as the total California income tax. And as we go down to the credits section of form 540, we see that there is a credit for other state taxes and code 187. That's just how you notate it on this form. But this number should look familiar. 5458. All right. And we can see that that's being subtracted out of the total tax liability. And what we're left with at the California level is total tax of 470. So what's happening is that the income was indeed taxed in New York and you pay taxes on that to New York at the state level. What California is doing is giving you a credit for that tax. So the California tax as we saw was 6,50 5928 if you take out the exemption and then we take out the taxes you paid to the state of New York to get the net California tax. So essentially you're getting a credit for the taxes paid to that other state. and California is allowing you to reduce dollar fordoll the state tax liability in California based on the credit that you're getting for those out of state taxes. So this is a very simple example. It can get a little bit more convoluted than this, but essentially this is the mechanics at work when you have income that you earned outside of the state in which you reside. And it's interesting because New York is a high tax state, but what can happen is if you're in a state that has a very low income tax, then you may still get the credit for the taxes you paid to that state in California, but at the same time, there's still going to be a lot of California tax that's going to be applicable. Very well be that the state tax credit paid to the other state was 200 bucks and you still have a significant tax liability. But here's where you'd look to see if you're getting credit for the taxes paid to that other state on the California resident return or wherever you happen to reside. And I mentioned at the very beginning, this does not apply in all situations. The reason I mentioned that is because some states do not allow such a credit depending on which state you reside in and which state you work in. And sometimes you may end up being double taxed depending on the state in which you live versus the state in which you work. Sometimes they don't play nice with each other. And if you look at the instructions to these forms, you'll see that some states say, "Ah, we'll give you a credit for these states, but we will not give you a credit for these states." So, believe it or not, it gets pretty petty at the state level as far as where you can and and and cannot get a credit for taxes paid to different states. So the point is in a good amount of situations you will not be double taxed because of this dynamic of one state giving you credit for taxes paid to a different state. And it may work out that you get a credit by your home state for taxes paid to the other state, but it can be the opposite as well in that the other state will give you a credit for taxes paid to your home state. So either scenario is meant to avoid double taxation. But just keep in mind that you may very well end up in a situation where at the state level you're taxed on both sides being the state in which you reside and the state in which you work because they're not playing nice by applying credits respectively. All right. So hopefully this video gave you some understanding of the mechanics of state tax credits and the fact that double taxation is generally not applicable at the state level, but it can be in certain circumstances. And if this was helpful, feel free to hit that like button, leave a comment, subscribe to the channel, hit that bell icon to be notified of future videos because there will be more. And aside from that, as always, hopefully this was helpful. Thanks for watching.

- The Best Tips for Getting an Out of State Job: Kind: captions Language: en Hey everybody, it's Andy back for another week to help you build a career you love. Today we're going to talk about how to get an out of state job, so what those best tips are. This is really your remote job search, so if you're like me and you're in the United States and you're looking to change states, these techniques will apply, but I've also used these tips for people who are outside the US who want to move to the US or want to change countries. These techniques will apply regardless, but it is about moving for a job. I'm assuming as we go through this that you've decided you want to move. This is not about a company who hired you who is going to relocate you. This is about targeting opportunities in another state that you would like to move to. It is more complicated than searching for an opportunity within your city. Let's run through these. I've got nine of them. Some are a little more static, some are a little more dynamic, but let's run through them. I think you'll enjoy them. The first thing is I would take your address off your resume. It's subtle and simple, but we don't need to confuse matters if you are sending your resume to a different city or a different state. I generally recommend that you have an address of some sort or you let the employer know what town or state that you're in, but in this particular case, I'm a fan of removing it. It will only cause you hardship to leave it on and reduce the chances that somebody's actually going to give you a call back for an interview. The first thing that I would do is I'd remove your resume. The second thing is along the same lines. If you have an opportunity or you know somebody in the city that you are moving toward, it is kind of nice if you can have a home base in the city you want to move to. I realize that that is not always the case, but tip number two, if you can get an address in the target city, that will favor you. I would put that on the address. Those are just some things that you can clean up on the resume. I also recommend on your LinkedIn profile putting the city that you want to move toward. It will help as recruiters are searching for candidates in their area. They're more likely to come across your profile if you have the city that you want to move in on the LinkedIn profile. Your LinkedIn profile is not a legal document. In this particular case, it's okay to give yourself a leg up. If you do get calls out of the blue from recruiters, it's always okay to say, "I put that on my profile because I have decided I am moving there, so I started my search." It's perfectly okay. That's step number three. All right, tip number four is I would start to put together a target list of companies in the city you want to move toward. I always recommend that no matter who you are, however junior or senior you are, that you drive your job search based on your targeted company list. It is never more important than if you're moving cities to make sure that you have a list of great companies that you can reach out to. If you want more information on how to put a target company list together, I did an entire live office hours on that. I'll put the link in the description so you can check that out. There's 12 steps to putting together a great target company list, so I would start targeting organizations in that area so you can connect up with them with your resume, your cover letter, your LinkedIn in mails and however you're going to reach out to those organizations, but I would definitely have a list that you are targeting. That's number four. Number five, I would then start looking at the job boards. I wouldn't just look for positions that are available in the target city that you want, but I also like using the job boards to evaluate which companies are hiring. Even if they don't have a position that suits you perfectly, if the organization is hiring people, it's usually an indication that it's in growth mode and it would be a great company for you to reach into with your resume and your cover letter. I've also provided some free cover letters about how to apply when there's no job opening. You can check those out, but I would use the job boards to make sure that you're also servicing positions that are available, but also companies that are hiring to augment your targeted company list. That's number five. Number six, I recommend using LinkedIn to do some online networking with people in that particular city. Start following companies in that city. Start looking for people who work at organizations in that city. Start reaching out to them. You can say, "Hey, I noticed you work for this organization in such and such a city. I'm moving there and I'm trying to foster relationships with people who are there. I'd love to know how you feel about the city, your organization," and so on. It's okay to be upfront and honest, but I would start to plant the seed with people who are in that area and see if you can strike up a relationship with them. You never know. They might be able to turn into an employer referral for you or at least give you some insight, locations to live, organizations that are hiring, whatever it might be. You'd be amazed at what the locals know, so I would use LinkedIn to do that. Tip number seven, I would look at any meetup groups or online groups or organizations within that city that you might be able to join, learn, start networking with people in that town. In addition to LinkedIn, I would still look for online networking groups or other types of groups where you can get connected. Maybe if you're going to take some visits, you might plan them around meeting dates, things of that nature, but that's another great way to give you a leg up on targeting opportunities in a new city. That's number seven. All right, so number eight. This is really where the rubber meets the road. This is as you start to communicate with the organizations in that target city. You've looked at your target companies. You've got your city that you want to move to. You're going to start reaching out to them, whether you're applying directly through email or through the applicant tracking system. When you get your cover letter together, there are some messages that have to be conveyed in the cover letter, but before we go into any of those, I want you to understand the recruiter or the HR person's mindset when they're looking at somebody from out of town. Sometimes, not always, but sometimes they're concerned about the relocation expenses. They might not be able to afford the additional expense of moving you from a different city, so that's one thing that some of them might be concerned with. All of them, they're always concerned with whether you actually will follow through with the move. An out of town employee is a much greater risk than somebody that lives in the city, so you need to put those fears to bed. When you put your cover letter together, you need to be very explicit on a few things. "I'm reaching out because I want to work for your organization and I've targeted your organization because it's a great one and I have determined that I am moving to your city for sure." You've got to leave no doubt. You can't make it conditional, like, "I will move if I'm offered a job." It has the same effect that they're just as concerned about the relocation expenses and all these other things that are going to come up. It needs to be solid, and you also might want to include your rationale for why you are moving to that city, to the extent that you can add in extremely solid reasons, "I'm moving there for family. I'm moving there for friends. My wife or my husband is getting relocated because of their job." Whatever it might be, if you have that at your disposal, I would put it in the cover letter because that will help them rest easy that you are truly coming. The other thing that you might want to put in there is that you are coming no matter what and you are happy to pay your own relocation expenses. The most important thing is that you want to get with a great organization, which you think they are, and so on. You can check out some of my other cover letter templates that you can use, but you want to make sure that you are adding these elements in because you want to diffuse those issues. You do not want the employer worrying about that as an out of town person who's going to relocate. Number nine, I would also be very flexible on the interviews. To the extent that you can, accommodate their phone screen. Knowing that you're out of town, they're likely going to screen you on the phone. They might do a video interview and after a couple of sessions, they're going to want you to come in and be as flexible and accommodating as you can. If you want some tips to handle the phone interviews and the video interviews and those kind of things, I'll put those links in the description here as well so you can get some tips on how to interview effectively. Those are the nine tips. I know that it's a little bit more complicated. - States with the Most Human Resource Manager Jobs: Kind: captions Language: en-US Where are the best places in the US to find HR Manager jobs? For other industries, everyone knows the destinations that aspiring professionals head to. If you want to get into movies, many head to Hollywood. Those wanting to get into fashion head to New York. For internet technology, you might move to Silicon Valley. But what about HR Manager jobs? Well, let’s take a look right now! Hi Streamliners, Greg here with another video on HR careers. Today, we’re asking where can we find the largest amount of HR Manager jobs in the country? In some industries, where you live can give you a significant advantage by providing more opportunity. Other industries are a little more spread out and the location doesn’t always matter. But what about HR Manager roles? Does it matter where you live? If you are a college student graduating with a human resources degree, or maybe an HR professional looking for the market that might provide you with the most opportunity to land that next position, this variable can have an impact on your job search. Now to be clear, HR manager roles exist all throughout the country. While many think of HR roles as tied to corporate headquarters, they are often found at many businesses that have lots of employees. In manufacturing or processing plants for example. Warehouses, hotels, distribution centers, you name it, there may be an opportunity for human resource jobs to be available. However, living in a town with only a few large business locations may severely limit your opportunities and could create a risk, should you get laid off. So the Bureau of Labor Statistics provides an analysis by job category of prevalence in each market. They look at both state and local markets. Today, we’re going to focus on the states where the most opportunities exist. The current data is taken from their 2019 survey. While the data is not perfect, the BLS analysis is a great resource. As you can see from the map, they track how many “HR Manager” roles are available in each state. Now to be fair, the number of opportunities is strongly correlated with the state’s population. For this video, states with more people and more jobs will tend to have a higher count of HR folks on staff. That said, I will also share the employment per thousand jobs and the location quotient, which is the ratio of area concentration relative to the national average. As a bonus, we’re going to identify the top five states/districts for HR Manager jobs relative to location quotient. So stick around until the end for that. On the map, there are 14 states that listed as dark green, which means that there are greater than 3,850 HR Manager jobs in the state. Those states are • California • Florida • Georgia • Illinois • Massachusetts • Michigan • Minnesota • New Jersey • New York • Ohio • Pennsylvania • Tennessee • Texas • Virginia On the flip side of this, in 2019, there were only 160 HR manager roles reported in Wyoming. North and South Dakota reported 240 and 270 HR Manager roles, respectively. So while there are a few HR jobs in those states, there aren’t many. Well then, now that we know the top fourteen, and the bottom three, who are the top five states? Coming in at number 5 is Florida at 7,980 HR Manager roles. While the Employment per thousand is a little lower at 0.91 and the location quotient is a mere 0.86, the volume of jobs is high, which helps catapult Florida into the top five. Coming in at number 4 is Texas with 10,260 roles. Similar to Florida, the employment per thousand jobs is quite low at 0.83 and the location quotient is a mere 0.78. Despite these lower ratios, Texas has the population to have over 10,000 opportunities for HR Mangers. Coming in at number 3 is Illinois with 10,890 roles. With a strong employment per thousand of 1.81, Illinois is a state well represented by HR professionals. The location quotient is also a strong 1.71, which is the highest of any of the states in the top five. Coming in at number 2 is New York with 10,980 HR Managers. New York has a health 1.15 roles per thousand jobs and a respectable location quotient of 1.09. New York has a lot of HR opportunities within it’s market. And number one on the list is … drumroll please … given my clues earlier, you can probably guess it is California, with a staggering 21,600 HR Manager jobs. California has a healthy 1.24 HR manager opportunities per thousand jobs and a location quotient of 1.18. So population and strong quotient numbers drive California to it’s number one ranking. So I kept referencing jobs-per-thousand and location quotient. Now for extra credit, which are the top five states or districts with the highest concentration of jobs? Surprisingly, this is almost a totally different list. But if you are looking for the states with highest concentration, it actually shakes out as follows: • Coming in at number five is Minnesota with a 1.58 employment per thousand and a location quotient of 1.5. • Connecticut comes in at number 4 with a 1.8 employment per thousand and a location quotient of 1.7. • Appearing on it’s second list of the night is Illinois at number 3 with a 1.81 employment per thousand and a location quotient of 1.71. • Massachusetts comes in at number 2 with a 1.86 employment per thousand and a location quotient of 1.77. • And the District of Columbia comes in at the top of the list with a 2.21 employment per thousand and a location quotient of 2.09. I hope this video provides you with some insight on how HR Manager jobs are distributed around the US. Please don’t take this list as a direction that you need to pick-up and move in order to find an HR job. It is simply intended to help people understand where a specific job tends to be most prevalent. Also, I did not speak to salaries in each of these markets. That will be presented in a later video. - What Are Residency Tie-breaker Rules In Tax Treaties For Dual Residents? - Asian American CPA: Kind: captions Language: en What are residency tiebreaker rules and tax treaties for dual residents? Imagine being caught between two countries, each claiming you as a resident for tax purposes. It can be confusing, but there are clear rules to help decide which country gets to tax you. When someone qualifies as a resident into countries under their domestic laws, tax treaties step in to resolve this. These treaties include specific rules called residency tiebreaker rules. They are designed to assign a single country of residence for tax purposes, so you avoid paying taxes twice on the same income. These rules are usually found in the treaty's article about residency, often called article 4. The process involves a series of tests that are applied one after the other. First, they look at where you have a permanent home. If you have a permanent home in only one country, that country is your tax residence. If you have a permanent home in both countries or neither, they then check where your personal and economic ties are strongest. This includes your family, work, social connections, and financial interests. If they still can't decide, the next step is to see where you habitually live, meaning where you spend most of your time. If you are still considered a resident in both or neither country, they look at your nationality. If you are a citizen of only one of the countries, that can determine your residency. If all these tests don't give a clear answer, then the authorities from both countries will talk to each other to reach an agreement. For example, the United States and China have their own versions of these rules. The United States applies these tests in order while China prefers to consult directly with the other count's tax authorities. For Asian-American taxpayers, understanding these rules is very helpful. Many immigrants or expatriots may have ties to both the United States and an Asian country, leading to dual residency. Properly applying these rules helps avoid paying taxes twice and clarifies your filing obligations. When claiming treaty benefits based on these rules, you need to notify the Internal Revenue Service by filing form 8,833, which is the treaty based return position disclosure. Not filing this for might cause the IRS to treat you as a US resident, which could lead to double taxation or other issues. Even if the treaty assigns you to one country, the other country might still tax income earned within its borders. However, the treaty usually provides relief like exemptions or foreign tax credits. For Asian-American taxpayers, navigating dual residency, working with tax professionals who know both US and Asian country laws and treaties is a smart move. They can help you correctly apply the tiebreaker rules and meet all reporting requirements.

- How Do Tax Treaty Residency Tie-breaker Rules Work For Dual Residents? - Asian American CPA: Kind: captions Language: en How do tax treaty residency tiebreaker rules work for dual residents? Imagine being caught between two countries, each claiming you as a resident for tax purposes. It can get confusing, especially if you have ties to both. When you are considered a resident of two countries under their domestic laws, tax treaties step in to help. These treaties include rules called tiebreaker rules to decide which country gets to treat you as a resident for treaty benefits. They are mainly found in article for of most tax treaties. The process starts with the permanent home test. This checks where you have a permanent place to live. If you have a home in only one country, that country is your tax residence for treaty purposes. If you have homes in both or neither, the next step looks at where your personal and economic ties are stronger. This includes your family, social life, work, political or cultural connections. The country where these ties are closer is usually your residence. If that still doesn't give a clear answer, the treaty considers where you usually live or spend most of your time. This is called the habitual abode test. If you spend more time in one country, that country may be your tax residence. If this also doesn't resolve the issue, the next step looks at your nationality. If you are a citizen of only one of the countries, that country is often considered your residence for treaty purposes. In rare cases where none of these tests work, the tax authorities from both countries will work together to decide your residency. They will look at your specific facts and circumstances to make a decision. For AsianAmerican taxpayers who have ties to both the United States and an Asian country with a tax treaty, understanding these rules is very important. Even if you are considered a resident in both countries under their laws, the treaty tiebreaker rules help determine which country has the primary right to tax your worldwide income. This way you avoid paying taxes twice on the same income. To claim this, you usually need to file form 188333 with the Internal Revenue Service. This from shows you are claiming treaty benefits and helps prevent being treated as a US resident for tax purposes. If the treaty assigns residency elsewhere, remember these tiebreaker rules only apply for treaty purposes. They do not change your domestic residency status. You might still have to file in both countries, but the treaty helps you avoid double taxation. If you are a green card holder or meet the substantial presence test in the United States, claiming foreign residency under the treaty can impact your US tax status and rules about expatriation. Overall, these rules give a clear process to decide your tax residency when you have ties to more than one country. They help reduce confusion and prevent double taxation. If you have complex residency situations, it's a good idea to review the relevant treaty and consult a tax professional. They can help you navigate these rules and make sure you are compliant while optimizing your tax position.

Your citizen country will definitely agress you on tax. You need to be really picky on building up your residence proof in a way that you are going to exclude falling in this trap. To make the story short either you are really living and having solid tight in the country you want to be tax resident or the outcome could be unexpected. Digital nomads citizens of countries with world wide taxation scope hardly can get away on that case.

- DTAA Explained in 5 Minutes for NRIs | Double Tax Avoidance Agreement: Kind: captions Language: en double taxation avoidance agreement which is dtaa is a treaty between two countries aimed at preventing double taxation of the same income in both countries thus promoting economic activity this agreement ensures that clients are not tax twice on their income to understand their tax liabilities it is crucial for nris to understand how DT works so let's get started the double taxation avoidance agreement dtaa provides relief through two methods the exemption method and the tax credit method let's understand these methods in detail exemption method this method basically grants exclusive taxation rights to one country while the other country does not tax the income it is usually used when the NRI earns income from dividends interest and royalties tax credit method in this method both countries have the right to tax the income but resident country offers a tax credit for taxes paid in the source country it is usually used for salary and business incomes I will explain how this method work with an example in case of exemption method suppose yush who is a resident of UAE and earns dividend or interest income from his investments in India both countries have a dtwa agreement in place now in case of exemption method India where the the interest income is generated charges tax on this income whereas UA being the resident country of yeses exempts this income from taxation since it's already taxed in India as a result yes doesn't pay taxes on this interest income in UA let us now consider a similar scenario but apply the tax credit method so again Yash who is a resident of USA earns interest income from investments in India now India taxes this interest income at a rate of 10% now when yes files taxes in USA he declares the income earned in India USA allows yes to claim a tax credit for the taxes paid in India so Yash receives a credit equal to the taxes which is 10% paid in India for his tax liability in USA if the tax rate in USA is higher than 10% yush may have to pay the difference if it's lower he won't owe any additional taxes so dtwa works on these two methods however to claim dtwa benefits an NRI must follow these steps the first step is to understand the dtwa agreement between India and your country of residence the agreement will detail how different income types which is salary or interest are taxed in each of the country the agreement also tells us whether the dtwa uses exemption method or a tax credit method second step is to gather the required documents these are the documents that an NRI must have to claim the DTW benefits the document usually are as follows a self declaration or an indemnity form now this is a legal document declaring your eligibility for the DTW benefits a copy of pan card issued by the Indian tax department a copy of Visa which verifies your resident status in the foreign country Pio proof if applicable and copy of a passport to verify your identity and nationality lastly also an NRI will have to submit a foreign residency proof by applying for a TRC this TRC which is tax residency certificate is a crucial document issued by your foreign country tax authorities proving your residency status an NRI can obtain it using form 10 FAA and form 10 FB after verification please note that this document needs to be renewed annually for more information on TRC you can have a look at my previous video now step three is to claim the dtaa benefit by submitting these documents all nris must provide the TRC an indemnity form to the tax withholding entity in India to claim a lower tax deduction at source which is lower TDS if claiming the tax benefit during tax filing they must submit all the documents to the Indian tax authorities along with their tax returns the nris must also note that specific documents and processes might vary depending on the dtaa and individual circumstances so make sure that that you consult a tax adviser or a chartered accountant familiar with the DTW and personalized guidance by understanding the dtwa and Gathering the necessary documents nris can efficiently claim their dtwa benefits and avoid double taxation on their income so that's all for today's episode if you find this information helpful subscribe to our Channel and stay informed see you again in the next video
