So today, we'll be talking about, um, sort of data in action, um, how we translate data into public health practice. Um, next slide, Dennis. So, my name is Hannah Peterson. My pronouns are she and her, and before we get too deep into this, I do want to take a moment to acknowledge the moment that we're living in. Um, there have been recent announcements of large funding cuts to public health, um, and I know that many of us are carrying uncertainty. And I… I think it makes the conversations that Dennis and I are going to have today about, sort of, purpose and responsibility and impact really meaningful. Um, so I thank you, uh, for continuing to show up in this work right now. It takes intention and a whole lot of grit, um, and I'm grateful that you're here today, so… Uh, but before you meet me professionally, I want to take a moment to introduce myself personally. Um, I like to say that I have grandma hobbies, uh, which means that I love to read. I read, uh, 65 books last year. Um, I love to garden, um, you can see in this first photo, uh, I… there was an early freeze one, uh, winter, and so I brought all my potted plants inside. Um, that is the Covehead Lighthouse, so if you know, you know. Um, and I love to eat! Which means I love to cook, and I love to make, um, and I particularly love when all of my hobbies intersect. Um, and you can see in this last photo, uh, Eric and I are in a gelato stand in Dubrovnik, Croatia, in front of a sign that says, a day without ice cream is a day wasted, and if you take absolutely nothing from it, this talk, uh, Let It Be That. Uh, next slide. So, my personal journey to public health started, um, during my undergrad at Seattle Pacific University. My very first class was Diseases, Plagues, and Pandemics, where I learned when an epidemiologist was for the very first time. Uh, that led me to, uh, get my master's degree at Tulane University School of Public Health and Tropical Medicine, uh, where I concentrated in chronic disease epi and biostatistics. Um, after graduating, um, I ended up at Denver Health's Rocky Mountain Poison and Drug Center, where I managed their pediatric acetaminophen misuse and abuse study. And now I'm at, uh, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment, where I've been for the last 6 years. Uh, my current role, um, is as a chronic disease epidemiologist. So, that means that I provide epidemiologic support to the diabetes, cardiovascular health, Alzheimer's disease and related dementias program, As well as the state physical activity and nutrition, uh, work that happens as a grant from the CDC. Um, a part of that work is the built environment and the healthy communities projects. Um, I also serve as a epidemiologist for the grant program, um, CCPD, which stands for Cardiovascular Disease, Cancer, and Pulmonary Disease. And I'm also the major architect of our chronic disease and health behaviors dashboard called Vision. If you've ever used that. I'm your girl behind the scenes, so… But at its core, my job breaks down into these four main things. Um, collecting data, managing it, analyzing it, and sharing it back out. And all of these things matter, and we've… and you've spent the last two weeks learning about specific components in many of these categories. But today, I really want to focus on that very beginning of that process, because I don't feel like we talk about it enough. So, today, I'm going to focus on data collection, and I think when most people think of data collection, they think of the technical components of collecting data. They think of surveys, they think of Excel spreadsheets, um, they think of SurveyMonkey and REDCap and all of these technical pieces of gathering data and holding onto it, but data collection is more than just this technical step. Um, it's more than a tool. It's this bridge to understanding, compassion, and progress. And I think when we think of data as a bridge, it reminds us that real people are on the other side of every single one of these numbers. I was chatting with a colleague a while ago about how the best ways to prove the effectiveness of a food as medicine program. And I was really focused on the data, and she was really focused on the people, and she was getting really frustrated with me, and she said, Hannah, people are not their biomarkers. And it was this really beautiful reframe that data serves as this bridge between connections and understanding, Between systems and people, and between what we know and what we do next. So if we treat data as a bridge rather than a tool, then the most important part of my job becomes deciding the best way to build that. bridge. Next slide. And for me, that bridge building starts the same almost every time, uh, with a question. Um, so almost every day, somebody reaches out with some curious… curiosity of, Like, do we have data on this? Or can you pull data for, um… Just this week alone, two of my latest questions are, do counties with high rates of wildfire smoke exposure also have high rates of asthma? Or, one of my favorites, do providing community dinners for men improve connection and reduce social isolation? And can they also reduce heart disease? So before opening a dataset, I have to pause, right? Because if you view data as a bridge rather than a tool, my job isn't just answering their question. Because we're already thinking of the datasets you would use to answer the first question, or the survey you would write. To answer the second. But it's about translating these questions or curiosities into meaningful research questions, and deciding whether data can responsibly connect what someone wants to know with the decisions that they plan to make, or the story that they plan to tell. So the answer for me to every question is usually another question. People who have worked with me will know that I will always want to meet with you and talk about your question. Because I want to understand what you're actually trying to know. And in my experience, the first question that people ask is rarely the question that they actually need the answer to. And if I were to jump straight to the data, treating data as a tool, I'll likely give you something that's technically correct, but probably not useful. So, to get to the real goal, I don't ask epi questions, like, it sounds like you're trying to understand the burden of disease, or… Will you be wanting a trend with that? Because we'll get there in our discussion, but in these early phases, it's important to understand the systems involved the people, uh, affected, and what stake. So instead, I like to ask questions like, what will knowing the answer to your question help you accomplish? Or, if you had the answer in your hands right now, What decision would it help you make? And sometimes you learn that people are looking for data for data's sake. they're kind of lost in the next step, and they just think, if I know this thing, maybe that will be… the catalyst for moving forward. sometimes people have money, and they're looking for guidance on where they can fund specific work. Um, I get a lot of questions specifically from my granting program of how do we know we're putting our money in the place of the highest need? Sometimes people are writing grants, and they need background information to support work in a specific area. One of… two of my favorite questions to use are, what story are you hoping to tell with this data? Or better still, what story do you hope the data tells you? Because in their own words, I want to know what connections they hope to make, and I want to know if they have an idea of where this data is going to take them. Next slide. So, good epidemiology starts with listening. I think of listening as this very first analytic step. I'm not just trying to understand what data they want, I'm also trying to understand why they want it. I think particularly in this time, listening is a form of justice, because data collection is power. the data we share depends on the priorities of our audiences, right? Policymakers and healthcare administrators care about money. But community workers care about people and their experiences. So data collection is the means of determining which problems are visible, which communities are centered, And which solutions feel possible? And that's power, and with it comes responsibility. So asking questions like the ones on the previous slide, they give me insight into how much someone has thought about their need for this data, how they plan to use this information, what connections they hope to make, and how they plan to move this work forward. And all of this context shapes every decision that comes next. I really think of it as this invisible work, right? It happens before you open a spreadsheet. It's that project management, it's the meetings, it's clarifying goals, it's aligning expectations, it's writing and rewriting the story. It's not time wasted, because it helps you set expectations early and prevents rework or misinterpretation of results later on. And this pre-work really helps you ensure that the data actually supports the decisions being made, And that the people represented in the data are treated responsibly. So now that I know, a question, I understand their goal, and we both feel good about the story that we're writing together. All of this work then shifts into the project management and all of the other sort of quote-unquote epi tasks. Like, this is the part… that people think of when they think of epidemiology. They think of gathering the data and braiding it together, and building graphs and dashboards and writing narratives. And all of it matters. But none of it works well unless, um, you've had that time investment to gather the context. So what does the day in the life of an applied epidemiologist look like? It looks like living in this cycle. It looks like listening, deciding, connecting, and executing. And sitting in each one of these buckets multiple times a day, across various projects. Because it's not just about the numbers and the charts. It's listening carefully and deciding responsibly, and connecting data to real people and real decisions. Because when we treat data as a bridge, with listening as that first step, Epidemiology becomes less about what we can measure, And more about how we choose to show up for the communities that our work represent. So with that, I'll pass it to Dennis, who's going to show you what it looks like when all of this comes together. Thank you very much, Hannah. - How to Read Your Insurance Card and Summary of Benefits - Health Insurance: Kind: captions Language: en Hey team, I am Ellie E. Um, before we start, heads up. Erica is taking her rescue dog to the vet for surgery. So, she has asked me to run the show today. Every insured American has a plastic card in their wallet and received a document called the summary of benefits and coverage when they enrolled. Most people have never read either one. Today, we are fixing that because those two items answer 90% of the questions people call their insurer about. Last episode, we decoded the metal tier system, bronze through platinum. Today we go one layer deeper into the actual documents that tell you what your specific plan covers and what it costs. The summary of benefits and coverage or SBC is a standardized 8-page document. The Affordable Care Act requires every plan to provide in plain language. >> I NOVA the cost of not reading these documents is real. People show up to a specialist without a referral because they thought their plan was a PO when it is actually an HMO. They get balance build because they did not verify the hospital was in network. From a provider side, the insurance card is the first thing a front desk checks. The card tells them your insurer, your plan type, your member ID, your group member, your co-ay amounts, and the prior authorization phone number. If any of that information is wrong or outdated, claims get denied before a doctor ever sees you. >> The SBC standardized format is actually a process win for consumers. Because every plan must use the same structure, you can compare a plan from one insurer to a plan from a completely different insurer by going to the same page in both documents. The method is consistent by law and that consistency is exactly what makes plan shopping less overwhelming. >> Before we open the topic, a fast round so you know everyone. >> Nova here. The numbers usually tell you where to look first. >> Marcus here. I will help you set a pace you can sustain to the end of the video. >> Isabella here. A good narrative outlives the slides. >> Shen here. method is a teachable skill or I will teach it. >> That is the lineup. Opening the book. Let me walk through every field on a standard insurance card. The member ID is the most important number. Every claim, every prior authorization request, every pharmacy transaction uses this number to connect the service to your specific policy. Lose the card? Screenshot the member ID first. The plan type field is often abbreviated HMO, PO, EPO, or POSOS. That two to threeletter code tells every provider whether you need a referral, whether out of network care is covered, and whether a primary care physician gatekeeper is involved. It is arguably the most consequential field on the card because it determines your access rules. >> Many cards print your co-pay amounts right on the front. primary care visit, specialist visit, and emergency room. These numbers tell you what you owe at the point of service before any deductible math applies, assuming your plan uses co-pays for those services. If the numbers on the card conflict with your SBC, the SBC governs the pharmacy section is easy to miss. It typically lists a pharmacy benefits phone number plus three codes, B I N, PCN, and RX group. These codes are what the pharmacist enters to process your prescription claim. If the pharmacist says your insurance is not on file, reading these numbers aloud from the back of your card solves it in about 30 seconds. The claims address and prior authorization phone number on the back of the card are the two numbers you call before a major procedure. Prior authorization is the insurer's pre-approval process. Skipping it means the claim could be denied entirely, not just at a higher cost sharing rate. Isabella, note that the prior O number is different from the member services number on the front of the card. Where do you actually find the SBC? If you enrolled through your employer, it should be in the benefits portal your HR team sent you to during open enrollment. If you enrolled through the marketplace, it is available on your insurers's website under your plan details. You can also call and request a paper copy. They are legally required to mail it within seven business days. SH and SASBC is your snapshot. It lists all the major covered services and shows your cost sharing for each one, deductible, co-ay, or co insurance, and whether the service counts toward your out-ofpocket maximum. The format is a table and every insurer must use that exact table. So comparing two SBC's from different companies means reading the same rows in both documents. The excluded services section is the one most people skip and it is the one that creates the worst surprises. Common exclusions include adult dental and vision, long-term care, cosmetic procedures, weight loss surgery, and some plans, experimental treatments, and acupuncture, depending on the insurer. Reading this section before you need a service is infinitely better than reading it after a denial. The coverage examples are a clever tool. Every SBC must include two standardized scenarios, having a baby and managing type 2 diabetes. These scenarios show exactly how much the plan would pay and how much the patient would owe for a fictional year of care. They are illustrations, not promises, but they make cost sharing math concrete in a way a percentage column cannot. Every SBC comes with an attached glossery of health coverage terms. This glossery has standardized definitions for premium, deductible, co-ayment, co insurance, out-ofpocket maximum, allowed amount, and about 30 other terms. If you encounter a word you do not recognize, that glossery is your first stop. Stick around because the last two concepts are where most people actually lose money. When you are comparing two plans using their SBC's, where do you look first? The deductible and the out-ofpocket maximum. These two numbers define your financial exposure for the year. Your deductible is the threshold before the plan starts sharing costs and your out-ofpocket maximum is your absolute worst case. Burst. Second, check the network type on each card and in the SBCC. This is the access question. An HMO with a fantastic deductible is useless if your primary care physician is not in that network. An EPO might save you money on premium but leave you uncovered for any care outside the network. Third, if you take regular prescriptions, look up your medication in the plan's formulary. Every plan publishes a formulary, which is a tiered list of covered drugs with different cost sharing at each tier. A medication that costs you $20 per month under your current plan could cost 200 under a new plan if it lands in a different tier. Chen, this is the one most people forget to check during open enrollment. Fourth, look at the out of network rules. Many plans, including PPOs, have a separate and much higher out-ofpocket maximum for out of network care. Some plans do not count out of network spending toward the in network maximum at all. If you travel frequently or have specialists in different cities, those outof network rules can represent thousands of dollars in unexpected exposure. The comparison method itself matters. Pull both SBC's and read the same labeled rows in sequence. First the cost sharing table, then the excluded services, then the out of network rules, then the coverage examples. Because the format is standardized, this sidebyside reading gives you an applesto apples comparison that premium sticker prices never will. The third document worth your attention is the provider directory. Every insurer publishes a searchable list of the doctors, hospitals, labs, and imaging centers that are in network for your specific plan. This directory is available on your insurer's website. Before you book any appointment, check that directory first. Here is the thing about provider directories that nobody warns you about. They go out of date. A physician can leave a network on January 1st and the directory might not reflect that change for weeks or sometimes months. Imagine a patient who checks the directory in October, sees her specialist listed as in network, enrolls in the plan, and then shows up to an appointment in February to find that the specialist left the network in December. Nova, the fix is to call the provider directly. Do not rely solely on the directory. Call the provers's billing office and ask two questions. Are you accepting new patients under my plan name? and are you currently in network for that plan? A good billing office will confirm both in about 60 seconds and save you potentially thousands of dollars in out of network charges. The financial risk of skipping the step is real. On most plans, out of network care means you pay a much higher co insurance, often 40 to 50% of the allowed amount, and the provider can charge above the allowed amount, leaving you with a balance bill. Your insurance card will not warn you that the doctor just left the network. That is your job to verify. >> The three-part verification process is the method. Confirm your card is current and correct. Read the SBC before using a service and verify the provider is in network by phone before any appointment. Three steps. Each one takes under 5 minutes. Together, they eliminate the most common and most expensive coverage surprises. If you forget everything else in this episode, remember your insurance card and your SBC together answer 90% of the questions people call their insurer about. Read both before you need either one. >> Drop in the comments, had you ever actually read your SBC before today? And what did you find in it that surprised you? >> Okay, it is quiz time. Let's find out what stuck. Think through each question carefully before you answer. >> If you are studying from us, the best study buddy you can be is a liker and a subscriber. >> Which section of the Affordable Care Act of 2010 required insurers to provide a standardized summary of benefits and coverage to enrolles? The correct answer is A. Section 2715 of the Public Health Service Act incorporated through the Affordable Care Act of 2010, which mandated a uniform plain language format up to eight pages. Why is the plan type field on your insurance card so important to verify when you start using healthcare. The correct answer is B. It tells you and your providers whether you need a referral to see specialists, whether out of network care is covered at all, and which rules govern your access to care throughout the year. What is the most reliable way to confirm that a specific doctor is currently in network for your health plan? The correct answer is D. Call the provider's billing office directly and ask if they are currently in network for your specific plan because online provider directories often go out of date when physicians change network contract. Here is your takeaway. two documents, your insurance ID card and your summary of benefits and coverage. Together answer nearly every question people have about what their plan covers and what it costs. Read them before you need them. This 3 years from now, you will thank yourself for spending 20 minutes with your SBC during open enrollment. The people who do that are the people who never get surprised by a medical bill. And why for many people that restriction actually saves more money than any other option. Subscribe so you do not miss it.   16. @gerardinachica: Working on a construction site isn't for everyone. There are many risks and hazards: the work environment, the equipment, the materials, the tasks, the workers, the schedules, the hours, and even company management are all potential sources of danger. It's simply the nature of the business. As the principal contractor or employer, it's your responsibility to ensure the prevention of work accidents and occupational diseases on the job site. This is not just because it's the law, but also because these accidents can have a significant impact on your productivity as well as on your workers and their families.
Managing occupational health and safety means implementing a prevention program - an action plan adapted to your reality. As a member of the [insert organization name here], you have access to a number of existing prevention programs in every type of construction trade. Each program has its own set of action sheets. Each sheet identifies a risk that could be present on your job site, and for each risk, there are details of what preventive measures and control methods you can put in place to protect your workers.
A prevention program is a great starting point, but managing occupational health and safety is a never-ending process of continuous improvement. You must continually identify, correct, and control all hazards in the workplace.
If you hold off on identifying the hazards until it's time to start the job, you can count on unwelcome surprises that will prevent the job from getting underway. What you need to do is identify the hazards during the planning stage.
When identifying a hazard, the most important thing to do is analyze each aspect of the work to be done: the equipment and materials to be used, the tasks to be performed, the work environment, and yes, even your workers. Two good tips for identifying hazards: one, consult your register of accidents, incidents, and first aid; and two, listen to what your workers have to say.
Once you've identified the workplace hazards, it's time to take action and correct the situation. Start with the hazards that can have serious and immediate consequences on your workers, such as those considered zero tolerance. Then correct the most frequent hazards, and finally, all other hazards.
You can correct the situation by putting in place corrective measures that eliminate hazards at the source, minimize the likelihood of accidents, or lessen the severity of potential injuries. There are many solutions to choose from: you can substitute materials or equipment, implement engineering controls, increase awareness, introduce administrative controls, or provide personal protective equipment. It's often necessary to combine several solutions in order to ensure effective results.
For each solution that you select, it's important to first establish a timeframe and designate a person in charge before implementing the corrective measures.
Once the hazards have been corrected, it's time to put the controls in place. This step helps ensure that the chosen solutions are both effective and sustainable. Because even if the chosen solution is the best one, it might not be properly understood or enforced by your workers. So it's important that you properly inform and train your workers and establish a monitoring system to ensure that the new controls are enforced.
The prevention programs provided by [insert organization name here] are indispensable when it comes to planning a safe and secure job site. But remember, every job site is unique, and workplace hazards can change and evolve as construction progresses. That's why it's important to follow the identify, correct, and control process to ensure proper management of occupational health and safety. This will help reduce work accidents and occupational diseases, and increase productivity. And that will ensure you avoid poor management that can lead to work stoppages, notices of correction, or even statements of offense from the CAN assisted. Prevention is a team effort and everyone's responsibility. It never ends. And remember,  advisors are always here to help

- Why Dual-Income Households Still Struggle With Housing: Kind: captions Language: en For decades, the idea of a dualincome household was supposed to make housing easier to afford. Two salaries, two paychecks, more financial stability. But for many American professionals today, even households with two incomes are finding that housing costs still feel increasingly tight. Mortgage payments, property taxes, insurance, and maintenance are quietly absorbing a larger share of household income, even when both partners are working full-time. For a long time, the dual income household represented financial stability. Two working adults meant more purchasing power, more flexibility and monthly budgeting, and greater resilience when costs increased. But in many parts of the United States, housing expenses have evolved in a way that is quietly reshaping that equation. Today, many dual income households are discovering that two salaries no longer create the financial breathing room they once did, particularly when it comes to housing. The issue isn't always dramatic. Mortgage payments are one part of the equation. Over the past several years, mortgage rates and home prices have moved significantly. Even households that purchased homes during lower rate periods often experience increases in related property taxes often adjust based on home value assessments. As property values rise, those tax bills tend to increase as well. At the same time, homeowners insurance has become a growing part of housing expenses. Insurance premiums have steadily increased due to rising repair costs, weather related risk, and broader insurance market adjustments. For a dual inome household, these increases may seem manageable at first. After all, there are two paychecks supporting the household budget. But the structure of modern household finances means that housing costs often expand alongside income growth. When two incomes become the baseline requirement for maintaining a mortgage, housing expenses become anchored to maximum earning capacity. In other words, the system begins to assume both incomes will always be present. That assumption can quietly change the financial dynamics of home ownership. For example, when one partner experiences a career pause for caregiving or job transition, the household may feel the full weight. Housing costs typically behave as fixed expenses. Mortgage payments remain constant monthtomonth. Because of this structure, housing expenses rarely adjust downward when income growth slows. Another factor shaping the dual income housing dynamic is location. Many professionals live near employment centers where demand is strong. This means even households earning two professional salaries may find themselves dedicating a large portion of income toward housing alone. In previous decades, a single income could often support housing. Today, dual incomes are no longer an advantage. They are a requirement. Home prices and financing costs have gradually aligned with the assumption that many households now have two earners. Two incomes once expanded financial freedom. But in many housing markets today, they simply maintain financial balance.

- Do Sellers Still Pay the Buyer’s Agent? New Rules Explained for 2025: Kind: captions Language: en Back in August of 2024, all of the rules regarding real estate commissions changed to a certain extent. And even though it's been several months since that has happened, there's been a ton of videos published and there's been a lot of reporting in the media about this. There is still a great deal of confusion. And I'll admit, it's not just confusion amongst potential buyers and sellers. There is also confusion amongst different brokerages because everyone is handling it slightly different. But over the past few months, a couple of the most common questions I have been getting from sellers has been, does this mean that we no longer have to pay the buyer's agent? Is buyer agent commission done away with now? And from a buyer's perspective, it's been, so since this changed, does this mean if I want to use an agent that I now have to pay them directly myself out of pocket? And honestly, the answers might surprise you. So, in this video, I'm going to break it all down for you. I'm going to tell you what's actually changed, what hasn't, and what it all means for you in practical terms and in what I'm seeing every single day so that you can get a better gauge and idea of what's actually going on in the market. So, let's dive into it and clear up the confusion. Hi, this is Andrew with the Andrew Smith team expy here in Frisco, Texas. And first of let's start this video out by talking about what exactly changed and the essence of what was behind the lawsuit. So boiling everything down, what basically happened is the terms of the settlement were that there can no longer be a unilateral offer of compensation. So what in the world does that mean? So, let's go back and look at how things were in the past. As a seller, you're planning on selling your home. You meet with a listing agent and a commission amount would be decided upon. A commission amount would be agreed upon. And that amount, whatever it was, was then split between brokers. Part of it was going to the listing brokerage representing you, the seller, and part of it was being made out or being offered out, I should say, as an offer of compensation for the brokerage that brings in the buyer. That is no longer allowed. There can no longer be offers of compensation made in the multiple listing service. And the idea behind that is it was felt that it was unfair to sellers and it was unfair to buyers because the there was there wasn't a whole lot of transparency on the buy side on potentially what they were making or what was being offered. Were agents intentionally only taking buyers to properties that were offering a higher fee for instance? So it wanted to take all of that away which I'll be honest with you is a good thing. that brings transparency back into it. So everything's on the table. So in essence, what they did was uncouple commissions. The buyer is responsible for negotiating their fee with their agent should they use to use one and the seller is responsible for ne negotiating their listing fee with their agent representing them in the sale. That in essence is what changed. Okay, that is what is and isn't allowed anymore. So, how does this actually play out in terms of a buyer and a seller? What should you expect? So, that's what I'm going to get into right here for you. So, let's start with the seller side. So, first and foremost, on the seller side, it doesn't mean that you no longer will be asked to contribute or pay a buyer's agent commission. It just means that you're no longer required to offer it. And it's now a strategic decision, not an automatic one. So, what exactly does that mean? Well, we've all been there. I mean, you know, you may have a home that's listed for 500,000 and you're offering a percentage out and the offer comes in low, right? It's lower than what you want and then there's these fees involved. You're looking at all of this. All of that is out the window, right? Or it should be out the window. As I said, there's confusion amongst brokerages still on this as well. But it is very likely and very possible that the buyer is going to ask you for a closing cost credit to cover all or part of their fee to their that they're paying the buyer's agent. So the commission to the buyer's agent, but it's not a fixed amount. It's going to be negotiated through the actual offer. So, if you get a really good strong offer and they're asking for a concession to cover that fee, you know, that could still work. That could be something that you choose to do. Also, from the seller side, it's also important to understand that for certain buyers, it is possible that you will reduce the buyer pool if in fact you just unilaterally say, "No, I'm not offering anything out." Why is that? Well, there's many buyers that can afford the monthly payment on a house, but they're cashstrapped. You know, they have enough money for a down payment, but they don't have enough money for a down payment and all of the fees that typically go along with it. And if it's a buyer that is in that situation and you're not willing to consider a concession towards covering their closing costs and or their agent fees, then that buyer financially simply might not be able to move forward with the purchase of your home. But again, it is now 100% negotiable offer by offer. All right, so hopefully that clears it up a little bit from the seller perspective. All right, now for buyers, what changed with buyers? Well, there's more changes on the buyer side than there was on the listing side. On the listing side, there's really not any changes except the discussion that a listing agent and the seller is going to have with regards to whether or not they're going to consider covering part of buyer agent fees. The bigger changes are on the buyer side and more than me, the commission one is the one that says a buyer representation agreement must be signed prior to that agent showing you any property. And I'll admit, I don't like it. I don't like that change. I don't think it's a fair change. I don't think it achieves a necessary goal. Let's face it, buying a house is expensive, right? and it's the biggest financial investment the vast majority of people ever make in their life. And now an agent that you've spent 30 seconds or a minute speaking to on the phone. Um, you know, you want them to go and show you a house and now you've got to sign an agreement, commit to them and commit to paying them a fee if it's not going to get covered by the seller. Doesn't seem right. Doesn't seem fair. And I agree, but that's the law. That's what the law actually says. Now, in order to stay true to the law and also be fair to buyers, a lot of different companies do different things to accommodate this. You are not going to be locked into or you shouldn't be locked into or you should never agree to be locked into a long-term agreement with an agent. You don't know. You don't know anything about them. You don't know if they're any good. You don't know if you get along with them. And then you're going to agree to let them help you with this. No. What you can do is there are ways where you can sign an extremely short-term uh showing agreement. In essence, it could be for one day, it could be for one property, it could be for one weekend. It's fully customizable and it expires quickly and you can revoke it if you determine that it you and that agent are not a good fit. So what has happened now is as a buyer you do have more options. So if you you know you can choose to hire a full-ervice agent. You can choose to use an agent in a limited capacity. Maybe you've bought several homes before and you know your way around and you're comfortable doing some of the heavy lifting yourselves and you want to negotiate uh a fee with an agent to possibly handle inspections for you, make sure the contract is correct, review the comparables, help you with closing documents, things along those lines. You can hire an agent to do just that. You could also choose to represent yourself. You could choose not to have any agent. You could choose to do all of it your own and or with the use of an attorney or you could contact the listing agent directly. Now, I will tell you or I will advise you in my opinion that is not your best option. Um, and I'm not saying that to try and say an agent should be involved. That's not what I'm saying here at all. You need to do what's best for you. But the rules and the laws in different or they vary from state to state. And when you have a situation where you're contacting the listing agent directly, I want you to keep in mind that the listing agent works for the seller. Right? So again, I'm not going to speak for other agents or other people, but I'm going to tell you from my perspective. This is the way I work. If I'm the listing agent on a home and you're an unrepresented buyer and you want to see that home, I will meet you at the house when I can and I will show you that home. I will unlock it. I will make the appointment. I will get you in and you are more than welcome to look around. If you want, if you decide it's the absolute perfect home and you want to make an offer, you have a couple of choices. Again, you can hire a real estate attorney. You can hire an agent of your own at that time or you could ask me to write the offer for you or any listing agent for that matter. But keep in mind that that agent, I'm going to speak for myself here, right? I represent the seller. And since I represent the seller, I cannot represent you. It would be a purely transactional relationship between you and I. What does that mean? It means I can't advise you on the price that you should offer. I can't advise you what you should do in terms of ex inspections. I can't advise you with regards to questions on certain disclosures or title forms or things like that. I can't offer you any advice or guidance because that would be a conflict of interest. My job is to protect the interest of the seller. So hopefully that makes sense. Could you potentially save some money that way? Possibly, absolutely. But that's a decision that you need to make on your own. So, how do I recommend you go about it? If you wanted to see a property, I recommend that if you're thinking that you might want to use an agent that you go ahead and sign a short-term representation agreement specific to one house, one day, one weekend, something along those lines. Get to know each other. go on a date. In essence, see if you like each other, see if you think you could be someone that you work with. And during that time frame, you can ask a few more questions about how that agent works, what's going to be involved, what type of services do they offer. From there, it's always possible that you then schedule a second meeting. And in that second meeting, you would go over it almost like a listing agent gives a presentation to a seller and goes through everything. It would be the same thing. you would meet with that agent and go through everything and how they work and negotiate the fee that the agent is going to make completely negotiable between you and that agent. So hopefully that clears it up a little bit with regards to what changed on the buy side. So all of that being said, in practical terms, what is actually happening? Are sellers still covering the buyer agent commission? And I will tell you, at least in this market, the answer is yes. For the most part, they most definitely are. Now, it's just done different. It is not against the rules or the law, per se, that agents can't email agents or send flyers out or put riders on signs with a specific offer of compensation. The brokerage I work with doesn't believe in that. they don't believe that's the intent of the law. So, I usually have this discussion with a seller, explain what their options are, and then just say, depending upon the offer, would you be willing to contribute towards buyer agent compensation or commission? And the overwhelming answer has always been yes. In which case, the only field in our local MLS that talks about this is one that says seller concessions, and it is simply a yes or a no. If we put yes, you can know that the seller is willing to consider compensation. And what I tell agents when they call me is whatever percentage or amount you have agreed upon with your buyer, go ahead and include that in your offer and it will be negotiated just like all of the other terms of the offer. But overwhelmingly I am still seeing the sellers contributing and covering that fee for the simple reason is that we are in a much more competitive market these days. I can see if this was the market like what we had in 2021 then no sellers were not going to cover that fee. They didn't have to. That's when multiple offers and over bidding were taking place and things like that. Now in this market that's not the case. It's much more balanced. It's much more normal. So sellers are motivated to sell and they know that things are taking longer to sell. So they are more inclined to offer buyers concessions, whether that be towards closing costs or whether that be towards the buyer's agent commission in either way. Also, if the buyer is submitting a strong offer overall and it's a very reasonable offer, most sellers are still willing to contribute. I will tell you, if we look at the Pacific Northwest, so I know Washington, they they haven't offered buyer agent compensation or commission percentage in the MLS for a long time. And I don't know the exact percentage, but it's still over the past few years, it's like 98 or 99% of all the transactions, the seller is still covering the fee. So again, hopefully that helps. Now, what does this mean going forward? What should you expect? Well, you know, I don't think any of this is a bad thing. It's just made the conversation much more transparent, right? And it's going to force agents to prove their value. For years and years and years, let's face it, you know, the idea behind it was, well, for a real estate agent, you know, they don't really do anything and then they get paid this huge fee. And the people that make that statement are usually people that haven't been a real estate agent and actually done it. And I know I could get flack for that and that's fine, but it's not quite as simple or as easy as people make it out to be. However, what this does is there's nothing hidden. Everything's on top of the table. What the fees are are presented upfront. They're clear. Everybody knows about them, both buyer and seller. So, adding the transparency into it is a good thing overall. I also suggest for buyers and sellers that, you know, before you're ready, you start interviewing agents early. understand exactly what it is that they're going to be doing for you and you can make the decision then on whether or not you are going to utilize the services of an agent. I mean, let's face it, when selling your home for years, you've had the option to put a sign in your yard and have it for sale by owner, there's been nothing restricting you from doing that. Well, according to Zillow's analysis, it's about one in 13 home shoppers. That's nearly 8%. So, we're not talking about a niche little thing here. This is a real emerging trend. कमर्शियल या रेंटल प्रॉपर्टीज़ को शामिल किया होता, तो उससे रियल एस्टेट रिटर्न्स बहुत ज़्यादा हो जाते। और क्योंकि ज़्यादातर लोग एक घर किराए पर ले रहे हैं और फिर उसकी तुलना रेजिडेंशियल रियल एस्टेट से कर रहे हैं, तो हमें शायद रेजिडेंशियल रियल एस्टेट मीडियन होम प्राइसिंग का ही इस्तेमाल करना चाहिए। सरलता के लिए, अगर स्टॉक्स औसतन सात प्रतिशत प्रति साल का रिटर्न देते हैं, और रियल एस्टेट औसतन दो प्रतिशत प्रति साल का, तो अंतर लगभग पाँच प्रतिशत है। यानी, स्टॉक्स रियल एस्टेट से पाँच प्रतिशत प्रति साल बेहतर प्रदर्शन कर रहे हैं। अगर आप एक लाख डॉलर डाउन तो ये वो एक लाख डॉलर हैं जो हर साल पाँच परसेंट ज़्यादा कमा सकते थे अगर आपने उन्हें स्टॉक मार्केट में इन्वेस्ट किया होता। ये वो पैसा है जो आप कमा सकते थे अगर वो इस घर में इन्वेस्टेड नहीं होता। तो, हम जल्द ही यहाँ कुछ असली नंबर्स डालेंगे। लेकिन पहले, मैं बस इस कॉन्सेप्ट को समझाना चाहता था। ये कॉस्ट ऑफ कैपिटल या मॉर्गेज पेमेंट के कॉस्ट का पहला पार्ट है। अगर आप यूएस में नहीं हैं, तो ग्लोबल या अपने देश के रियल एस्टेट रिटर्न्स गूगल करें, और उन्हें S&P 500 या अपने स्टॉक इंडेक्स से कंपेयर करें। मैंने पाया है कि स्टॉक्स आमतौर पर रियल एस्टेट से बेहतर परफॉर्म करते हैं, देश के हिसाब से, लगभग तीन से पाँच परसेंट तक। कॉस्ट ऑफ कैपिटल इक्वेशन का दूसरा पार्ट वो कॉस्ट है जो इंटरेस्ट पेमेंट्स आपको कॉस्ट कर रही हैं, या, AKA, इसे कॉस्ट ऑफ डेट के रूप में जाना जाता है। आज के सिंपल कैलकुलेशन के लिए, हम मान लेंगे कि ये अट्ठाईस हज़ार डॉलर प्रति साल है। हम बस लोन बैलेंस टाइम्स प्रति साल का इंटरेस्ट रेट वाला सीधा मेथड इस्तेमाल करेंगे। और तो, ये हमें एक बहुत ही कंज़र्वेटिव बफर देगा ये पता लगाने में कि हम किस तरह के घर अफोर्ड कर सकते हैं और बस वो कौन से कॉस्ट्स हैं जो हमें वापस नहीं मिलते। ठीक है, तो अगर आप लोग अभी भी फॉलो कर रहे हैं, तो कॉस्ट ऑफ कैपिटल है डाउन पेमेंट की अपॉर्चुनिटी कॉस्ट, जो हमने पता लगाया था कि एक लाख डॉलर का पाँच परसेंट है। वो है पाँच हज़ार डॉलर प्लस अट्ठाईस हज़ार डॉलर इंटरेस्ट पेमेंट्स में प्रति साल। ये टोटल तैंतीस हज़ार डॉलर का कॉस्ट है हर साल जो हमें वापस नहीं मिलता। परसेंटेज के रूप में, हम तैंतीस हज़ार डॉलर को घर की वैल्यू से डिवाइड करेंगे और वो छह पॉइंट छह परसेंट के बराबर होता है। अब जब हमारे पास वो सारे नंबर्स हैं, तो चलिए उन चीज़ों को जोड़ते हैं जिनकी हम पहले ही बात कर चुके हैं, प्रॉपर्टी टैक्स, मेंटेनेंस कॉस्ट, और कॉस्ट ऑफ कैपिटल, और हम एक सिंपल रूल पर आएँगे। उम्मीद है कि फॉर्मूला यहीं स्क्रीन पर होगा। और अगर नहीं है, तो आप मेरे एडिटर, रिकी को ब्लेम कर सकते हैं। आप उसे कमेंट्स में बता सकते हैं। मज़ाक कर रहा हूँ, वो शायद इसे देख लेगा। तो ये है, एक पॉइंट एक एक परसेंट का प्रॉपर्टी टैक्स, प्लस एक परसेंट मेंटेनेंस कॉस्ट, प्लस छह पॉइंट छह परसेंट कॉस्ट ऑफ कैपिटल, और हमारा टोटल है आठ पॉइंट सात एक परसेंट। हम इस आठ पॉइंट सात एक परसेंट को एक क्विक और आसान गाइडलाइन के तौर पर इस्तेमाल कर सकते हैं ये पता लगाने के लिए, कि घर खरीदने का कॉस्ट मंथली बेसिस पर कैसा दिखता है। यहाँ रूल ये है कि घर की कीमत लें, उसे आठ पॉइंट सात एक परसेंट से मल्टीप्लाई करें, और फिर उसे बारह से डिवाइड कर दें। ये आपके घर खरीदने का टोटल कॉस्ट है मंथली बेसिस पर। तो, अगर आपके एरिया में चार लाख डॉलर का घर है, तो हम उस चार लाख डॉलर के नंबर को लेंगे, उसे आठ पॉइंट सात एक परसेंट से मल्टीप्लाई करेंगे, और वो असल में आपको वहाँ घर खरीदने का एनुअल कॉस्ट देता है, जो है चौंतीस हज़ार आठ सौ चालीस डॉलर। अब, उसे बारह से डिवाइड करें और आपको मिलेंगे दो हज़ार नौ सौ तीन डॉलर। घर खरीदने के मामले में ये आपको मंथली बेसिस पर इतना कॉस्ट करेगा। तो, अगर आपके एरिया में चार लाख डॉलर के वैसे ही घर को दो हज़ार नौ सौ तीन डॉलर प्रति महीने से कम में किराए पर लेना सस्ता है, तो ये पलड़ा किराए पर लेने के पक्ष में भारी रूप से झुका देता है। मैंने गूगल शीट्स में एक बढ़िया कैलकुलेटर बनाया है जिसे आप फ्री में डाउनलोड कर सकते हैं। ताकि, अगर आप नंबर्स के साथ खेलना चाहते हैं, तो कर सकते हैं, लिंक नीचे दिया है। आप जिस घर को देख रहे हैं उसका प्राइस एडिट कर सकते हैं। वेरिएबल्स, अपनी डाउन पेमेंट आदि बदल सकते हैं। और कैलकुलेटर के आखिर में, वो आपको आपका ब्रेक-ईवन मंथली पेमेंट बता देगा। इस तरह, अगर मॉर्गेज रेट्स बदलते हैं या आप किसी दूसरे देश में रहते हैं, तो आप इस कैलकुलेटर का इस्तेमाल करके रेंटिंग वर्सेस बाइंग की स्थिति का अच्छा अंदाज़ा लगा सकते हैं। यह नियम बहुत सरल है और कई लोगों को पसंद आएगा, पर कुछ लोग इस पर आलोचना भी करेंगे। यह आपको मासिक आधार पर ब्रेक-ईवन पॉइंट समझने में मदद करता है, लेकिन इसमें कई कमियां हैं। कुछ ज़रूरी बातें भी हैं जिन पर हमें चर्चा करनी चाहिए जिनमें घर खरीदने का गणित शामिल नहीं है। पहली कमी जो मुझे दिखती है वो ये है कि इंसान अक्सर इर्रेशनल और कई बार इम्पल्सिव होते हैं। जैसे, कई बार मुझे पता होता है कि सात डॉलर की लाटे खरीदना कोई अच्छा आईडिया नहीं है, लेकिन मैं फिर भी जाकर उस पर पैसे उड़ा देता हूँ। सिर्फ इसलिए कि आपके कैपिटल का एक अपॉर्चुनिटी कॉस्ट कंसीडरेशन है, इसका मतलब ये नहीं है कि अगर आप वो डाउन पेमेंट नहीं करते हैं, तो किराएदार अपने पास मौजूद एक लाख डॉलर लेगा और उसे मार्केट में परफेक्टली इन्वेस्ट करेगा और अपनी अपॉर्चुनिटी कॉस्ट का फायदा उठाएगा। बेसिकली, इन्वेस्टिंग में बहुत डिसिप्लिन लगता है और आपको... काफी परफेक्टली खेलना पड़ता है। कौन कह सकता है कि वो किराएदार वो डाउन पेमेंट लेकर उसे ब्लैक पर नहीं लगा देगा, शायद जाकर 21 बना ले? 21 रेड? मुझे खुद नहीं पता मैं इस पॉइंट पर क्या बोल रहा हूँ, पर आप समझ गए। इंसान इर्रेशनल होते हैं और हमेशा परफेक्ट नहीं होते। तो, एक तो वो बात है। और फिर, मैं मॉर्गेज पेमेंट को एक तरह से फोर्स्ड सेविंग्स मेथड की तरह देखता हूँ क्योंकि आप अपना मॉर्गेज पेमेंट चुकाते हुए इक्विटी जमा कर रहे हैं? ये उन बहुत से अमेरिकन्स के लिए एक बहुत अच्छी बात होगी जो आम तौर पर सेविंग करने में बहुत अच्छे नहीं हैं। तो, आज इक्विटी बनाने के इसी टॉपिक पर, हमने इंटरेस्ट की कॉस्ट को बस मॉर्गेज रेट टाइम्स आपके लोन अमाउंट के रूप में बात की थी। असल में, मॉर्गेज की कैलकुलेशन इतनी सीधी नहीं होती। ये एमॉर्टाइज़ेशन शेड्यूल के हिसाब से चलते हैं, जहाँ शुरुआत में आप अपने मॉर्गेज पेमेंट का एक बड़ा हिस्सा इंटरेस्ट के तौर पर चुकाते हैं और इक्विटी में कम जाता है। लेकिन जैसे-जैसे आपका मॉर्गेज आगे बढ़ता है, समय के साथ, आप इंटरेस्ट में कम और इक्विटी में ज़्यादा चुकाना शुरू कर देते हैं। तो, बेसिकली, इस पॉइंट पर मुझे एक तरह से एहसास हुआ है कि आपकी कॉस्ट ऑफ इक्विटी और आपकी कॉस्ट ऑफ डेट हमेशा बदलती रहती है एमॉर्टाइज़ेशन शेड्यूल के वेरिएशन की वजह से। अगर आप इसे इस बात के साथ जोड़ दें कि, हे, पंद्रह साल के मॉर्गेज भी होते हैं, अलग-अलग तरह के वेरिएबल रेट मॉर्गेज भी होते हैं, तो हमारे पास बहुत सारे वेरिएबल्स हैं जिन्हें हम आज के सिंपल रूल में नहीं गिन रहे हैं। प्लस, तीस साल के आखिर में, इंटरेस्ट की कॉस्ट लगभग खत्म हो जाती है। उस पॉइंट पर लगभग सब कुछ इक्विटी ही होता है। ये आज हम जिस रूल की बात कर रहे हैं उसे काफी हद तक बदल देगा। और, साथ ही, ये खरीदने का रेंट करने पर एक और फायदा या अपसाइड है, जो ये है कि तीस साल के आखिर में, आपके पास असल में एक टैंजिबल एसेट होता है जिसकी वैल्यू समय के साथ बढ़ सकती थी। दूसरी तरफ, किराएदार हर महीने ये मंथली पेमेंट देता रहा है और वो उसके लैंडलॉर्ड की जेब और बैंक अकाउंट में जा रहा है और उन्हें उस पैसे का फल कभी नहीं मिल रहा है। तीसरी कमी जो मैं अक्सर इस रूल में देखता हूँ वो ये है कि ये हमेशा मान लेता है कि कंडीशंस बिल्कुल सेम हैं। इन्फ्लेशन किसी साल बहुत ज़्यादा बढ़ सकता है, जो... असल में बढ़ा भी है। या शायद आपका मॉर्गेज रेट डबल हो सकता है, या शायद आपकी इक्विटी की अपॉर्चुनिटी कॉस्ट बहुत अलग हो। ये निश्चित रूप से सोचने वाली बात है और इसीलिए मैंने कैलकुलेटर बनाया ताकि इसे हमेशा रिवाइज किया जा सके। आखिर में, हमने मॉर्गेज इंटरेस्ट डिडक्ट करने के टैक्स बेनिफिट्स पर तो बात ही नहीं की। तो, अगर आप यूनाइटेड स्टेट्स में हैं, तो आप अपनी इनकम के अगेंस्ट मॉर्गेज इंटरेस्ट डिडक्ट कर सकते हैं। अब, यूनाइटेड स्टेट्स में स्टैंडर्ड डिडक्शन तेरह हज़ार से चौदह हज़ार डॉलर प्रति वर्ष है एक व्यक्ति के लिए। तो, इसका मतलब है कि अगर आपके पास राइट ऑफ करने के लिए मॉर्गेज इंटरेस्ट के रूप में इससे ज़्यादा नहीं है, तो इसका कोई खास फायदा नहीं है। हालांकि, अगर आपके पास चौदह हज़ार डॉलर से ज़्यादा है, मान लीजिए, मॉर्गेज इंटरेस्ट में जिसे आप डिडक्ट कर सकते हैं, तो ये असल में मंथली बेसिस पर आपके घर के ओनरशिप की ओवरऑल कॉस्ट को कम कर देता है। आपको असल में ये नंबर्स खुद कैलकुलेट करने होंगे क्योंकि मॉर्गेज इंटरेस्ट और डिडक्शन के साथ बहुत सारे वेरिएबल्स आते हैं, जिसमें आपका टैक्स ब्रैकेट, आपका मॉर्गेज अमाउंट, और साथ ही आपका इंटरेस्ट रेट शामिल है। घर ओन करने के कुछ फायदे भी हैं जिनमें मैथ्स कैलकुलेशन बिल्कुल भी शामिल नहीं हैं! पहला ये है कि आपके पेमेंट्स प्रेडिक्टेबल और फिक्स्ड होते हैं, खासकर एक फिक्स्ड मॉर्गेज के साथ। क्योंकि आपका पेमेंट असल में बहुत ज़्यादा नहीं बदलता। आप असल में अपने मॉर्गेज पेमेंट के लिए सही-सही बजट बना सकते हैं। जबकि अगर आप किराए पर रह रहे हैं, तो आपके लैंडलॉर्ड किसी भी समय प्राइस बढ़ा सकते हैं। एक और फायदा यह है कि अपना घर होने पर आपके पास ज़्यादा कंट्रोल होता है। असल में, अगर आप अपनी प्रॉपर्टी में बदलाव या अपडेट करते हैं, तो लैंडलॉर्ड से पूछने की ज़रूरत नहीं। आपको अतिरिक्त सुरक्षा भी मिलती है क्योंकि लैंडलॉर्ड आपको सिर्फ इसलिए नहीं निकालेगा क्योंकि उसने अपनी प्रॉपर्टी किसी और को बेच दी है। मॉर्गेज ओन करने का एक और फायदा ये है कि इन्फ्लेशन आपकी मदद करता है। अब, मुझे पता है कि ये सुनने में थोड़ा उल्टा लगता है, लेकिन मतलब, समय के साथ उसकी कीमत कम होती जाती है। मैं एक एक्सट्रीम उदाहरण दूँगा, जो असल में नहीं है। मान लीजिए, पचास साल का फिक्स्ड मॉर्गेज था और आपने घर पचास साल पहले खरीदा था। अब, इससे पहले कि आप मुझ पर चढ़ें, मुझे पता है कि पचास साल का मॉर्गेज नहीं होता है और चालीस साल का मॉर्गेज भी बस एक मिथ है। लेकिन इस उदाहरण के लिए बस मेरी बात सुन लीजिए। तो, पचास साल पहले आपको एक पचास साल का फिक्स्ड-रेट मॉर्गेज मिलता है और आपका मॉर्गेज पेमेंट तीन सौ डॉलर प्रति महीना है। खैर, उस समय, तीन सौ डॉलर महीना आज के दो हज़ार डॉलर महीने जैसा था। हालांकि, इस फिक्स्ड मॉर्गेज पेमेंट की वजह से, आप लोन की पूरी अवधि के लिए अभी भी तीन सौ डॉलर महीना ही दे रहे हैं। और आजकल, मुझे आपका तो पता नहीं, पर तीन सौ डॉलर बहुत ज़्यादा नहीं लगते। ये असल में एक नॉर्मल तीस साल के मॉर्गेज के साथ भी होता है, बस एक छोटे, कम स्केल पर। और घर ओन करने का आखिरी और सबसे बड़ा फायदा ये है कि आपको अपनी होम वैल्यू में बहुत ज़्यादा कैपिटल एप्रिसिएशन देखने को मिल सकता है। मेरे एरिया के एक घर का ये उदाहरण देखिए जो इस साल की शुरुआत में बिका था। ये 1995 में पाँच लाख निन्यानवे हज़ार डॉलर में बिका था, और हाल ही में जनवरी में 3.23 मिलियन डॉलर में बिका है। इसने मीडियन होम प्राइस एप्रिसिएशन को कम से कम तीन गुना ज़्यादा आउटपरफॉर्म किया। होम ओनरशिप सिर्फ कॉस्ट्स और इन्वेस्टमेंट्स के बारे में नहीं है। अपना घर होना अपने आप में बहुत संतोषजनक होता है। और ये जानकर मन को शांति मिलती है कि रिटायरमेंट के लिए आपके पास एक घर है, खासकर जब आप इसे अभी ले सकते हैं, बाद में नहीं। मैं रेंटिंग के कुछ फायदे बताना चाहता हूँ। पहला है फ्लेक्सिबिलिटी। आप तीस साल तक एक घर से बंधे नहीं रहते। किसी वजह से, अगर आप अभी एक शहर में रहते हैं और अगले साल या महीने छोड़ना चाहते हैं, अपनी लीज़ जल्दी तोड़ना चाहते हैं, तो आप कर सकते हैं! अगला है, आपको टैक्स, मेंटेनेंस या ऐसी कोई लागत नहीं देनी पड़ती। जब आप किराए पर होते - Housing in Mexico: Why Renting is Smarter Than Buying! (10 Reasons): Kind: captions Language: en-US When someone talks to me about moving to Mexico, they'll mention culture, food, the beaches, people. But inevitably, a lower cost of living will always come up as a key reason. And it's true. As you saw in our last Mexico video, living in Mérida saves me a whopping $25,000 USD plus each year. Now, many expats see this savings and think, You know what? I want to lock in that savings, and I'm going to buy a house here in Mexico. I'm here to tell you that buying a property in Mexico is a terrible idea for most new expats. Buying a house here carries a ton of risk, and this risk is especially true if it's your first time living in Mexico. In this video, I'm going to run down the key reasons why new expats should avoid buying a house in Mexico and why renting is a better decision. If you miss out on this video, you might make a decision that will cost you tens of thousands of US dollars in the long run. And make sure you stick around for number seven because that one's a bloody shock. So grab a cerveza and let's get started. Hey, expats. I'm Marco Sison with Nomadic Fire with another fact-filled video guide to living your best life abroad. We bring you the best updated intelligence on expat life in different cities and countries overseas. With us, you get the unique perspective of having one source to compare, contrast, and catch life in the best retirement hotspots around the world. Here are the 10 reasons why renting is a better choice for expats than buying property in Mexico. First up is a lower initial investment. Renting typically requires a smaller upfront cost than buying. Most rentals only require the first and last month's rent along with a security deposit. Now, depending on the price of your rental, you're talking a few thousand dollars. Now, let's take a look of what happens if you're purchasing. We're talking about a significant expense. Besides potentially paying for a real estate attorney to review contracts, you're going to be paying close to 4% to 10% of the home's value in closing costs. Then if you decide to get a home, many Mexican lenders require 15% down payment, and then there are some lenders even demanding up to 30%. Here's your first insider tip, additional upfront rental costs. Depending on the city you're renting in Mexico, you may also be asked to provide an aval or a poliza jurídica. In Queretaro, for example, a poliza jurídica is common, but in Mérida, an aval is more likely to be required from. Now, you may be wondering what these words mean. An aval or a poliza are just different ways a landlord or a homeowner can protect their investment. An aval is typically someone you know in Mexico that owns a home and can be your guarantor in case you stop paying for rent. So if you leave the house in crappy condition, this person is going to be on the hook. Yeah, you may imagine being the new guy that just moved to Mexico and trying to a local friend willing to put their butt on the line for you like that is going to be pretty hard. Now, a poliza is a little easier. It's basically an insurance policy that protects both you and the landlord if you don't pay the rent or if you damage their house. Now, the good news is a poliza also protects you as the renter in case the house has any hidden defects or the owner reneges on their portion of the contract. The cost for a policy typically is 10 to 20% of one month's rent. If a landlord asked me for an aval, My initial negotiating position is, no, no way. I'm already paying a one or two month deposit, which should be plenty. But you should be prepared if you find a landlord that's going to be harder to deal with. If this landlord is going to be harder with the negotiation, might need to look at a different place. If their negotiating position is they're still demanding some insurance or additional deposit, I try to negotiate a poliza. They're easier than finding someone who's willing to put their property up as a collateral to guarantee that I'll make a payment. Reason number two, interest rates for home loans in Mexico are ridiculous. If you come from a low interest rate country like the US, you're going to be shocked when you see interest rates for foreigners in Mexico hit 9 to 12 %. Now, given that in the cities we were living in Mexico, the purchase prices for a two bedroom, two bath near the city center is around $250,000 to $350,000 US. You're going to be looking at a massive interest payment on your mortgage. I quickly ran the numbers through an online mortgage calculator just to get an idea. And wow, you would not believe I would be paying nearly $800,000 USD of interest for a 30-year mortgage if I bought a house here. Reason number tres. Lately, property prices in Mexico have been shooting up much faster than rents. Meanwhile, prices are cooling off in the US, but rents are still climbing. So when you compare the United States and Mexico, Mexico's price to rent ratio is generally lower, which means renting is a pretty good deal in many Mexican cities. For example, Mexico's price to rent ratio sits at around 157, while it's about 135 in the US. And this matches our experience, where we found a one bedroom housing in cities like Querétaro, Mérida, and Oaxaca, to be much less than the average price of a one bedroom in the US. If you want more tips like these, join our Expat Insights Facebook Group for your Insider's Guide to Living Abroad. Our community is where you connect, share, and discuss real expat life overseas. No clickbate videos or filtered Instagram post here. Just unbiased truth from people who made the leap. Click below to join because life's too short to wonder, what if? Reason number cuatro, better flexibility in renting. Renting allows for greater mobility and the opportunity to explore different areas in Mexico before committing to a specific location. We've already done test drives in living in Mérida, Querétaro, and Oaxaca, and this doesn't even count how many times we've changed neighborhoods in a given city once we realize that a specific barrio just didn't fit our lifestyle. Now, you don't get to really change your mind easily as a homeowner. Look at point 10 down below. Here's your next insider tip. Even popular expat hotspots like Mérida isn't for everyone. Now, I know, I get it. No destination is a perfect Goldilocks city for everyone, but this seems especially true for Merida. People from the US or Canada come here in December or January to escape that dismal cold winters in the US and think, Wow, Canada is so amazing. Let's move here. But they don't realize how ridiculously hot it gets here around May or June. Think about 100% humidity and 100 degree heat in a concrete city with blazing sun in no shade. While I don't have the numbers to back up how many expats leave Mérida, I know enough people to say that the number is not small. And this is not a put down on Mérida. I'm just making a point that renting gives you a lot more leeway to make adjustments if it's your first time living in a new city. If you're a homeowner, leaving just because you can't handle the heat, dust, or noise in Mexico is going to be much more complex. Numero cinco, less maintenance responsibilities. Renters should not be responsible for repairs or maintenance, which can be a of sling around the homeowner. Maintenance isn't just a matter of unclogging toilets or tightening leaky faucets. I mean, you're going to need to make major repairs on appliances, you're going to need to fix a leaky foof, or you may need to fix a pool that doesn't work in properly. As a general rule of thumb, real estate experts estimate annual maintenance costs for a house using 1% of the home's value. For a $250,000 USD home, that translates to an additional $2,500 USD of yearly repairs. As a renter, landlords typically handle these issues, allowing you to avoid the stress and unexpected repair costs. Now, notice I said should because you need to make sure how maintenance is clearly spelled out in your lease agreement. Number six. In the US, most home buyers argue that mortgage payments are fixed while rents can increase. However, homeowners do not consider maintenance and home repairs. Remember when I said that real estate experts estimate annual maintenance cost to be about 1-2% of the home's current value? Well, guess what? As home values increase, so do the costs of repairs. Now, as I mentioned above, renting can mean a lot less hassle. Our rent included weekly housekeeping, pool maintenance, bi weekly landscaping, and all utilities included except for electricity. And because of the next point below, if you're worried about increasing rental prices, just sign a long term contract and fix the price. Number seven, rental laws favor the tenant in Mexico. Unlike most US cities, Mexico has much stronger rental protection laws. As long as you keep paying your rent on time and don't violate your lease terms, your landlord cannot kick you out. In the US, breaking a lease usually involves penalties and potential legal fees, but you could theoretically move out any time in Mexico. Now, you would obviously lose out in your deposit, but the landlord is going to have little recourse in getting you to pay for the rest of the contract. This protection is why many Mexican landlords insist on getting notarized rental contracts or deposits or the avals or the poliza jurídicas that I discussed earlier, just to reduce some of the risk. Numero ocho. Renting is just a less complicated process. Now, this one isn't unique to Mexico. Just like in the US, the rental process is generally much simpler than purchasing a property, which is going to involve lengthy legal and financial procedures. We can find a house to rent, sign a contract, pay the initial fees, and move in the same day. However, the complexity of buying a property in Mexico is much more difficult. Choosing a house and closing on a house is typically measured in weeks or months, not days. Renting allows you to avoid the complexities of buying property and figuring out local real estate laws and regulations in a foreign country where English is not the legal language. Here's another insider tip - notarios. When it comes time to sign contracts, you may come across the term notario, which sounds very similar to notary. But unlike notaries in the US, Mexican notarios are specialized attorneys with much broader legal authority. They're actually considered quasi judicial officials in the real estate transactions. A notario here in Mexico is going to authenticate, transact, and record all official property ownership changes. However, while a notario is a real estate attorney, they are not your attorney. Their plan is to stay neutral. So if you want a lawyer that looks after your interest, you're going to need to hire a separate Mexican attorney to view all your real estate transaction and contracts. Number nine, renting is just a better investment. Remember point three where I talked about how renting is cheaper than buying? Well, you can take that savings and put it towards your retirement fund or other investments without tying the capital up in real estate. Especially if you plan on taking a home loan with 12% interest, you will likely get a much better financial return by saving that big down payment and investing it in S&P 500 ETFs. Remember that 15 to 30% minimum down payment most Mexican banks require on a 30-year home? Well, let's say that down payment was roughly $50,000 US dollars. If you had invested that $50,000 down payment into a simple S&P 500 ETF, you would have over $1 million. Here's the last reason. It's just an easier strategy. Selling a home in Mexico is much different than in the US. First of all, there's no MLS-like central database of all available properties for sale, so it's just harder to find properties and buy and sell. Second, in many Mexican states, you don't even need a license to call yourself a real estate agent? This is not like the US or Canada where agents have accreditation and training and licensing. Here, it's literally the Wild Wild West. Now, there have been some states in Mexico that have started licensing agents. Still, even the President of the Mexican Association of Real Estate Professionals admits that only 10% of the people who offer real estate services have any proper training, professionalism, or certification to advise on real estate investments. Basically, all of what I just said adds up to a difficulty in selling. The time it takes to sell a property in Mexico is typically measured in months. Now, if your circumstance has changed, since your job gets relocated or you have health concerns, or even it's just personal preferences, if you're renter, you can just typically end your lease early. However, if you own your home, you could be stuck for months and months paying for a mortgage while looking for a buyer. Look, I get it. Retiring in Mexico sounds terrific. You're already counting the money you're saving and tasting the pork carnitas taco juice dripping off your chin. But moving here is not a decision you make blindly. Renting in Mexico gives you better financial flexibility, more financial protection, and peace of mind that new expats need. So before you jump into buying a new property, take the time to explore, understand the local markets, and protect your nest egg. Now, before we sign off, why don't you comment below, are you team rent or team buy? I'd like to see which housing option you most gravitate towards. And next, hit that like and subscribe button to turn on the notifications so you'll be notified when we drop our next video on the biggest culture shocks we experienced adjusting to our life in Mexico. Until then, hasta luego from Mexico...

than 28% of your gross monthly income or your total income before taxes and your total household debt shouldn't exceed more than 36% of your gross monthly income this is referred to as the 2836 Rule now let me talk about that 28 part because it's really hard these days especially in expensive cities to hit that number sure you can stretch it to 29 or 32 or even 34 but the more you stretch your housing costs the more risk you are taking and you can become overwhelmed with expenses very quickly if something goes wrong like an unexpected repair or a job loss now let's talk about the 36 part if your total household debt exceeds more than 36% of your gross monthly income you might have difficulty getting a mortgage from lenders more importantly you're putting your household at risk too much debt can sink you it's like swimming with too much weight on your back so to see if you can afford to buy a house based on your income and debt let me break down the numbers for you total housing costs divided by your gross monthly income equals your monthly housing costs just multiply that number by 100 to get your percentage is it lower than 28% it should be let me show you an example let's say your total housing costs are $1,120 a month and you make $4,000 1,120 divided by $4,000 equals 2828 * 100 that's 28% now couple of things I want to point out here first of all that total number it says total housing costs are $1,120 a month total does not just mean your monthly payment if you own it's the sprinklers it's the occasional repair it's even the gas to get to Home Depot it's all of it okay second this number says 28% ideally you would be a little lower than that so you have some margin although again in high cost of living areas like New York LA or frankly a lot of cities these days most people have gotten above this next calculate your 2836 number see if your total monthly housing cost plus your total debt load would be lower than 36% of your gross monthly income that means factoring in things like credit card debt student loans car loans all of it the more debt you have the riskier it is to lend to you and that means you might not be able to get a home loan but more importantly you're putting your household at risk here's how to calculate it amount of monthly debt you owe divided by gross monthly income equals debt to income ratio multiply that number by 100 to get your percentage the lower the number is the better the 2836 rule says that you should ideally have no more than 36% of your debt to income ratio here's an example let's say that you're spending 28% of your gross income on housing and you want to figure out your 2836 number well to find out the 36 number we look at the rest of your debt say you're spending $1,000 a month paying off credit card debt making $75,000 a year or 6250 a month 1,000 divided by 6250 is16 multiply that by 100 that's 16% well guess what in this case you're already spending 28% of your gross income on housing when you add the 16% you have a 2844 number that's higher than 36% that means you're in serious risk this would be troubling if I saw this so I want you to calculate your own 2836 number now there are a few exceptions to this rule if you live in a high cost of living area like New York La SF again some people stretch that number even up to 34 35% but it becomes very very risky next if you have no debt like like no car payment no student loans no credit card debt you might stretch your housing number a little bit I would personally consider going to around 33% but I'm very conservative with my finances if your income is reasonably expected to go up soon such as a promotion again maybe you stretch the numbers a little but I would be very hesitant about doing this after all this you should   1. @momijidiary [1 likes]: the analogy doesn't sit right with me. when you consume food  you cannot re-sell it or rent it out. when you buy a house, you can rent it, sub lease it, or even sell it in the future. renting is good and all. I rented for years too. but depending on where you live buying a home can still be a good move. because your rent increases in price every few years. the amount of money you send on rent is not the same in the next 20 years. most likely it will double. of course owning a home has its share of expenses too like taxes and insurance and maintenance cost but when you get older you can sell it or live in it for free (granted your mortgage is already paid off by then) the likelihood of your home increasing in value can also be considered depending on where you live and the trajectory of the population (if it is increasing).

the question is not renting is better or owning a home is better. The question is what is the lifestyle you plan to live in the future.

  3. @dclikemtndew [1 likes]: You admit that the first 21 years of a mortgage you're paying more in interest than toward the principle, yet when you compare renting vs owning you look at an 8-10 year timeline. It seems like if you were looking at this objectively, you're anti-mortgage, not anti-owning. I know, you'd probably say "well, the average person has to get a mortgage to own a home", which is true, but there's smart and dumb ways to get a mortgage, if you make a large down payment, and pay extra toward the principle, and actually see it through to the end, you're better off owning in almost every case.
  4. @michaelterrien1649: Everything you say is true, but it's incomplete.  For example, you note that property taxes can go up.  Fact check, true.  But you ignore the fact that rent WILL go up.  No 25 year old is renting an apartment today and then paying the same rent for it 50 years from now.  You note (correctly) that mortgage interest is "throwing money away" to exactly the same extent that that rent payments are.  But rent goes up over time and mortgage interest goes down as you amortize your mortgage, eventually getting to $0 when the house is paid off.  I'm not saying renting isn't better in some cases.  It surely is.  But you can't fully analyze the relative costs and benefits just by considering the static snapshot you present.  You have to consider the costs and benefits over the course of a lifetime.   And a lot of the benefits of ownership are backloaded.  I'm 62 and considering retirement.  I would not want to be looking at a remaining lifetime of rent at this point.  A paid for home takes a lot of the stress (financial and otherwise) off at this stage of my life.  P.S.  That $2M home that rents for $5K/mo. that you started out with is a great example of a situation where renting might make sense.  But it feels cherry picked.  I'm not sure how many places in America offer that kind of ratio of rental cost to property value.  I live in a $1M home in Chicago.  When we bought it a couple years ago, we did consider renting instead.  Anything we could find that was remotely comparable was over $10K/mo.  That's only two examples from two of the many, many markets in the United States and I'm not honestly sure if either is representative of the norm.  But $5K/mo. for a $2M home sounds like an uncommonly good deal to me.

That's roughly $24 trillion in residential equity sitting in states where a serious economic downturn doesn't just lower property values, it erases them. Compare that to the 33% free fall in Nevada or 28% collapse in Florida. Oklahoma's housing market barely registered a correction. The state's constitutional reserve fund held 1.05 billion dollars as of late 2025. Roughly 10% of the annual budget sitting in reserve. When downturns force other states into austerity, Oklahoma has a buffer that keeps property tax rates stable. Oklahoma also produces over 130 million bushels of wheat annually and ranks as the fifth largest oil-producing state. Energy and agriculture create dual revenue streams that function independently of consumer confidence or stock market performance. If that sounds boring, good. That's not just a lifestyle perk. States with income taxes face a vicious cycle during contractions. The state produces coal, oil, >> natural gas, gold, copper, platinum, and palladium. Hydroelectric power generates 37% of electricity. Agricultural output exceeds $5 billion annually. Montana's population has grown 9.6% over the past decade, but growth is constrained by geography and limited housing supply. That constraint means prices hold even when demand softens. There's never enough inventory for a glut. The premium you pay for Montana property isn't speculation. It's scarcity. And scarcity holds value when everything else crumbles.
