- GCSE Chemistry - Carbon Footprints - Measuring Footprints | Reducing Emissions (2026/27 exams): Kind: captions Language: en In today's video, we're going to take a look at carbon footprints. And in particular, we'll cover why they're so hard to measure, how we can reduce them, and why this is often so difficult in real life. When we say carbon footprints, what we're referring to is the total amount of carbon dioxide and other greenhouse gases that are emitted over something's entire life cycle. And that something could be a product like your phone, a service like plane travel, or even an event like a festival. The benefit of working out the carbon footprints of these things is that we then have a better idea of how environmentally damaging they are and so we're better able to decide whether or not they're worth the cost. It also means that we can identify the most polluting activities and try to reduce their emissions. The problem though is that measuring carbon footprints is actually really hard and in reality sometimes impossible. For example, if we consider something relatively simple like your phone, we'd have to take into account a whole bunch of things like how all the raw materials were sourced, the manufacturing process required to put it all together, the total amount of power that it uses over its lifetime, and finally how it's disposed of. In addition to all of this though, there would also be lots of other things that we've missed out like the emissions produced from transporting all the different parts around the world. Even if we have to accept that we can't get it perfect, though, a rough calculation can still give us a good idea of which things are the worst emitters and allow us to compare them. And once we understand where all the emissions are coming from, we can make sensible plans to try and reduce those emissions. One way to do this is to use renewable energy sources like wind, solar or nuclear energy instead of using fossil fuels. Another option would be to use more efficient manufacturing processes which would use less energy in the first place and also produce less waste. Reducing waste is actually really important because waste is often broken down by decomposers which can release methane. Governments can also help, for example, by introducing new laws or taxing companies based on how many greenhouse gases they emit. There's also something called carbon capture technology available that can capture the carbon dioxide formed when we burn fossil fuels before it can escape into the atmosphere. It can then be stored deep underground in cracks in the rocks such as old oil wells where it can't escape to the environment. Unfortunately, despite all of these good ideas, actually reducing emissions is easier said than done. For example, renewable energy is often more expensive than fossil fuels. And currently, our entire economy is based on fossil fuels, which makes it really hard to change. Meanwhile, governments worry that if they prioritize the environment, the whole economy might suffer. To make matters worse, this makes international agreements really difficult because no country wants to agree to anything that might hurt his economy unless it's completely sure that all the other countries will do the same. It's not just governments that have a role in fixing things, though. Every individual needs to be responsible for their own consumption. and be willing to make changes to their lifestyle if we're going to have any hope of averting the worst effects of climate change. If you haven't heard yet, you can find all of our videos on our website, cognito.org. You'll also find questions, flashcards, exam style questions, and pass papers. And we track all of your progress so that you always know what to study next. So sign up for free by clicking here or browse our playlist here on YouTube.

- Measure and reduce your IT carbon emissions: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] now about three out of four executives cite technology as critical for their sustainability efforts the next question then arises how can organizations measure and minimize carbon pollution from it operations which is also known as greenhouse gas emissions well let's explore how to do so on google cloud first let's begin at the source aka the energy that flows through data centers and powers the compute resources organizations use electricity was historically sourced by burning fossil fuels but this has been changing over the last decade thanks to huge investments and accessibility in renewable and non-carbon producing energy like solar wind geothermal etc since 2017 google has matched a hundred percent of its global energy use by purchasing renewable energy and has mitigated the annual carbon footprint of our customers digital applications and infrastructure we are also currently working on the ambitious goal of operating a hundred percent of the time on carbon free energy by 2030 this means we will match our energy demand for every hour of every day in every region where we operate with carbon-free energy supply for now it's important for each of us to reduce our cloud-carbon emissions by making decisions on the time of day and the regions that power our resources and so for starters it's incredibly helpful to understand where your electricity comes from by using online tools like the ones our partner electricity map has made available for free the next thing is to calculate your organization's specific it-carbon emissions and for this google cloud customers can leverage the out-of-the-box carbon footprint dashboard in the console which is viewable by folks with billing admin rights it includes accurate charts and tables on total emissions by project by month by product by region etc this same functionality will also be available for workspace customers early next year with carbon footprint you can also optionally export the data to bigquery to perform custom analytics and visualizations and use this data for sustainability reporting requirements on scope 3 greenhouse gases auditing and as a friendly reminder there are three emission categories at this time called scope 1 2 and 3 to help enterprises measure their carbon impact and so scope 1 and 2 relate to emissions specifically controlled by an organization scope 3 are an organization's indirect emissions such as their suppliers activities or their consumers product usage or effects from transportation and waste disposal to just name a few now once empowered with our carbon data let's take action by diving into some strategies for reducing carbon emissions in your google cloud infrastructure for starters i'm happy to share that the easiest and most impactful emissions reduction can come from selecting regions powered by cleaner energy in your cloud projects google introduced a metric called the carbon-free energy percent or cfe to empower users to select regions with higher cfe percentages because they produce fewer carbon emissions in many of google cloud's products when choosing a region there's also a friendly low co2 icon in the cloud console this icon is also annotated in the locations page of the documentation for different products now if you are looking for proactive advice on what regions to use there's also the fantastic region picker tool that helps you compare priorities around lowering emissions versus pricing versus latency for example you can learn that by moving a workload from london to finland you can increase the carbon free energy percent by 23 and lower the price by 14 i really recommend you check it out when you have a chance and if your organization provides users with a choice to select cloud regions you can furthermore set organizational policies to restrict locations to low carbon intensive regions using low carbon mode or you can allow or deny specific regions in the policy value box when configuring the resource location restriction you do this by entering the in prefix and then a specific string for one or multiple low carbon locations the next recommendation is to avoid resources from running 24 hours a day especially for development and testing and so for example by shifting resources to operate 10 hours a day during five work days a week instead you can reduce your footprint by 70 which is an impactful and cost-saving move and on the same topic around scheduling programming workloads to run during the day when there's clean energy available like solar or wind is also very powerful for example checking the energy mix in the uk on electricity map at 7 pm shows its 284 grams of carbon dioxide equivalent per kilowatt hour of electricity however executing that same workload earlier in the day such as 1 pm can be 200 and so by shifting workloads that may have operated at night to run earlier like at 1 pm can reduce carbon emissions by up to 29 percent and of course in addition to intentionally shortening resource schedules you can also proactively delete unused vms optimize vm shapes as well as shut down inactive projects this is where the active assist tool really shines as it proactively suggests carbon reducing configurations along with other cost performance and security friendly actions this is thanks to machine learning there's also several low carbon architecture designs you can improve here's just a couple for example by using managed serverless products you can leverage their separation in storage and compute resources enabling to use the same storage space while auto scaling vms up or down based on traffic this also prevents incurring compute or storage costs from idle resources and by also refactoring monolithic applications into bite-size microservices you can gain many efficiency benefits this is because microservices operate as self-contained modular functions that have less dependencies they are easier to maintain and debug and offer more granular control over resource scaling and less tests and so in summary wherever you are on your sustainability journey you have tools you can use in google cloud such as carbon footprint regent picker or active assist that are out of the box which i have linked in this video's description along with other helpful strategies and friends if there's one main thing to remember the best way to reduce your day-to-day carbon footprint is by picking the lowest carbon intensive regions for more information and best practices please check out cloud.google.com forward slash carbon footprint cheers you

- What Small Actions Effectively Reduce Your Carbon Footprint? - Earth Science Answers: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] What small actions effectively reduce your carbon footprint? Imagine making tiny changes in your daily routine that could help slow down climate change. It might seem small, but these actions add up to a big difference for Earth's changing climate. When you turn off unused lights and unplug devices, you cut down on energy waste. Swapping out old light bulbs for energyefficient lead bulbs can save a lot of electricity over time. Lowering your thermostat by just a few degrees in winter or raising it in summer reduces heating and cooling energy use. Setting your water heater to around 120° F also helps save energy because heating water takes a lot of power. Improving insulation in your home keeps the temperature steady, so your heating and cooling systems don't have to work as hard. Installing solar panels or heat pumps can make your home even more energyfriendly. Though these might cost a bit upfront when it comes to transportation, choosing public transit, biking, or walking instead of driving alone cuts down on fossil fuel emissions. Car pooling with friends or family also helps share the ride and reduce pollution. Limiting air travel is another effective step because flying produces a lot of carbon dioxide per mile. Planning your errands so you do everything in one trip minimizes driving and saves fuel. Changing your diet can make a big impact, too. Eating more plant-based meals and cutting back on beef and lem reduces methane emissions, which are very potent greenhouse gases. Planning your meals and composting food scraps help prevent food waste, which produces methane in landfills. Reusing and recycling are simple ways to cut emissions. Using reusable bags, bottles, and containers reduces plastic waste and the energy needed to produce new items. Buying secondhand or choosing products made sustainably lowers the carbon footprint linked to manufacturing and shipping. Water conservation also plays a role. Shortening your showers and fixing leaks means less water needs to be pumped, heated, and treated, saving energy. When you combine all these small actions, they create a powerful effect. They help slow the rise of greenhouse gases in the atmosphere, which is key to protecting Earth's climate. Every little effort counts because it reflects an understanding that human activities influence our planet's climate system. By making these changes, you join a global effort to reduce climate impacts and help keep Earth's environment healthier for everyone.

apply to a hundred do I apply to 50 do I apply to 200 where do I even start and so one thing that can kind of help simplify it for some folks is to work backwards so again start at the number of programs you want to rank adjust that number to get an actual estimate of how many interviews you want to go on and then look at how competitive your application is to try and decide how successful your applications will be in terms of actually getting interview invites and use that as a rough estimate to determine how many applications you should submit at the end of the day of course the odds are in your favor if you submit more applications so I would always err on the side of being more conservative and submitting more applications than you think you might need because let's be honest - Raising Kids in Norway vs. USA: Two Systems, Two Childhoods: Kind: captions Language: en Raising a child is complicated anywhere, but the rules of the game can look very different depending on where you live. The United States and Norway are both wealthy nations, but their approaches to childhood couldn't be more different. If you're American and thinking, "Wait, they actually get paid to have kids?" Yes, we'll get there. And if you're Norwegian, you might be wondering, "How do people survive daycare costs in the US?" We'll cover that, too. By the end, you won't just know what life looks like for kids on either side of the Atlantic. you'll understand the philosophies, the policies and structures shaping every day of childhood. I did a video recently on giving birth in Norway versus the US. So, you can go watch that video here if you would like, here or here. I always forget which side. Go watch that video if you want a little bit more depth into giving birth in Norway and that whole experience versus the United States. I'm going to do a small recap of the year after birth. However, this video will touch mostly on years 1 and after. So, let's start with parental leave. This to me is the first real signal that a society sends about how it values early childhood. In Norway, parents can take up to 49 weeks at full pay or 59 weeks at 80% pay, and 15 of those weeks are reserved exclusively for the father. The policy encourages bonding, gender equality, and long-term parental involvement. Research shows that longer paid leave benefits child health, breastfeeding duration, and maternal mental health. In the United States, there's something called the Federal Family and Medical Leave Act, FMLA, and they offer up to 12 weeks of unpaid leave for eligible workers, but millions of parents are excluded from this. Paid leave depends completely on employer policy or state programs. As a result, many parents return to work almost immediately after childirth. So, if you're an American parent and you're wondering why this feels like a sprint, it's partly systemic. Norway designs the first year as societal investment. The US leaves it to family finances and employer generosity. Child care is the next major difference. So, this is moving into years 1 through 5. In Norway, almost every child attends something called Barnahaga. It's basically a public preschool program. Fees are capped at around 31,720 croner per year for full-time care. That's around $3,200. And subsidies reduce costs further for larger families. Curriculum in these programs often emphasizes social skills, outdoor play, and equality. In the United States, child care is largely private, often costing $15,000 to $25,000 per year for a full-time infant. Quality varies dramatically by region and income. Families without access often faced tough choices like high costs, reduced work hours, or leaving the workforce entirely. The big idea here is that Norway treats child care as a public good. The US treats it as a private responsibility. Norwegian families also receive something called Barnet. This is a monthly child benefit of 1,968 croner per child. It's about $200 per child. This is tax-free and it is paid until the age of 18. Add this to subsidized child care, municipal afterchool programs, and other targeted benefits. And the total support significantly reduces the financial burden of raising children. Think of it as society saying, "We can't raise your child for you, but we can help shoulder the cost." In the US, many families rely on tax credits or employer benefits. The support is more limited and less universal, so families bear a higher financial risk. Moving into health care in Norway, healthcare for children is universal. Doctor's visits, preventative care, mental health support, and basic dental care are all covered. Parents rarely worry about bills. In the US, access is insurance dependent. Families without adequate coverage face deductibles, co-pays, or delayed care. Research shows that unequal health access correlates with measurable differences in child health outcomes. Education is another window into societal priorities. In Norway, school begins at age six with focus on social competence, equality, and outdoor learning. Testing is limited, especially in the early years, and funding is centralized, reducing gaps between schools. In the United States, formal learning usually starts earlier. Standardized testing is frequent and school funding comes largely from local property taxes which creates stark inequalities and things like achievement and competition are highly emphasized. The big idea here is that Norway aims for equal starting points and the US aims for individual achievements. Moving on to freedom, safety, and independence. In Norway, children enjoy early independence. Children as young as seven are usually seen walking to school alone, playing outdoors, and navigating minor risks on their own. Social trust is high, and the environment supports safe autonomy. In the US, independence varies heavily by community. Fear of accidents, crime, or liability shapes parental choices. Supervision is more intensive and freedom is conditional. Parenting identity differs, too. In Norway, the system supports parents. You have career pauses. You have daycare access and community involvement normalize shared responsibility. In the US, parents often face high financial and social pressures. The decisions about work, child care, and extracurriculars are intensely personal, which can create stress and competition among caregivers. So, what do these differences add up to? In my opinion, Norway builds a childhood safety net. You have predictable costs. You have a shared responsibility. You have broad access and you have trusted independence. On the other hand, the US builds opportunity and flexibility. There's potential for a high achievement, but it comes with unequal access and higher financial and social risks. Both systems aim to raise thriving children, but they assume different solutions. One spreads the load across society, the other across families. Which would you prefer? A system that's going to cushion your fall or one that gives you a ladder and says, "Can you climb?" And maybe most importantly, is there a way to get both? Thank you all for watching this video. I hope you enjoyed it. I'll leave a list of sources down in the description box below of some websites and things that you can go for further research into this topic. And don't forget to like, subscribe, comment, share, all of those really nice things that help support me and my channel. I also have channel members so you can join. You can get some cool benefits like early access. We have a Discord chat. It's a lot of fun. All right, I'll see you on the next one. Bye. [Music]

- The TRUE Cost of Raising Kids in AMERICA: Kind: captions Language: en The true cost of raising kids in America. America's birth rate just hit a record low. We're now at 1.6 children per woman. You need 2.1 just to replace the current population. For the first time in American history, more women in their early 30s are childless than our mothers. This isn't an accident. This isn't a cultural shift towards selfishness. This is economics. I talked to 32-year-old Alex, who's decided she probably won't have kids. She's got $80,000 in student loan debt. She pays $1,800 a month for a one-bedroom apartment. She makes $65,000 a year in a city where that's barely middle class. I can't even afford to take care of myself, she told me. How am I supposed to take care of a whole other human being for 18 years? And here's the part that should alarm us. Alex wants kids. She's not choosing childlessness. She's being priced out of it. Because here's what nobody's telling you. It costs $310,000 to raise a single child in America from birth to 18. And that doesn't even include college. This is the true cost of raising kids in America. And it's breaking us. Let me show you what that actually looks like. Meet Sarah and Marcus, both 32, teacher and software developer, $95,000 household income. They want a baby, so they pull out a spreadsheet because that's what you do now. Year 1, diapers, formula, doctor's visits, $12,000 minimum. Then child care until age 5, $14,000 per year. That's 70,000 before kindergarten. Sarah realizes that's more than her entire salary after taxes. She'd literally be paying to work. Then the school years, sports fees, music lessons, smartphones, car insurance at 16. $310,000. That's a down payment on three houses. That's a medical degree. That's early retirement. But here's what that number doesn't tell you. There's another cost Sarah and Marcus can't quantify. Sarah's maternity leave, 12 weeks unpaid because America is the only developed nation without guaranteed paid parental leave. Three months no income. They'll drain their emergency fund and start over from zero. But the real cost, Sarah will likely never make principal. She'll miss the evening meetings, the weekend trainings, the networking events because she has to leave at 3:30 for daycare pickup. Economists call this the motherhood penalty. Women's earnings drop 4% per child. Men's actually increase the fatherhood bonus over a lifetime. That's not thousands. That's hundreds of thousands, maybe a million. And then there's the mental load. Sarah will remember the pediatrician appointments, buy the teacher gifts, organize the playdates, notice when they're running low on milk 24/7 for 18 years minimum. Unpaid project management. How do you price that? Now, let me show you two extremes. Jennifer, single mom, two jobs, $32,000 a year. She qualifies for Medicaid and WIC. But here's the trap. If she gets a raise, she loses the assistance. The math doesn't work. So, she stays poor on purpose because that's the only way to keep her daughter fed and insured. Then there's Amanda and Richard. $250,000 household income. For them, raising kids is an arms race. private preschool at $30,000 a year. SAT tutors at $200 an hour. Summer programs in Costa Rica. College counselors charging $15,000 to craft the perfect application. They'll spend closer to $750,000 per child. Both families, Jennifer and Amanda, are responding rationally to the system we've created. One is trying to keep her kid from falling through the cracks. The other is buying her kid a spot at the top. And the middle, Sarah and Marcus, they're getting crushed. They make too much for help, not enough to play the game. They're cutting coupons for violin lessons, driving used cars to afford the good school district, doing everything right, and still drowning. Want more stories like this? Subscribe for deep dives into the systems that shape our lives. Alex isn't alone. Millions are doing this same calculation. Student debt, rising rent, stagnant wages. The math doesn't work. 20 years ago, the average firsttime parent was 25. Now it's 30. Not because people don't want kids, because they can't afford them yet. And for many, by the time they can, it's too late. Meanwhile, other countries are actually helping. France subsidized child care. Sweden 480 days of paid parental leave. Germany monthly child allowances. Japan paying people to have babies. America, a $2,000 tax credit that covers 2 months of formula. Maybe we've turned having kids into a luxury good, something only the wealthy can comfortably afford, something the middle class sacrifices everything for, something the poor get punished for. We write think pieces about why young people are choosing not to have kids, as if an entire generation just suddenly stopped liking children. That's not what's happening. We've made it too expensive to build a family in America. Sarah and Marcus are still sitting at their kitchen table staring at that spreadsheet. They'll probably have one kid. Maybe they'll make it work somehow. Skip vacations, drive old cars, and it'll be worth it because they'll love that kid more than anything. But they'll also lie awake wondering if they're doing enough, if they can afford the college fund, if a second child means bankruptcy. And that's the real cost we don't talk about. It's not just the $310,000. It's the stress, the impossible choices, the talented women who leave their careers, the dreams deferred, the siblings never born. It's a generation who wanted to be parents but couldn't afford to be. So in the richest country in human history, we've decided raising the next generation, the literal future, is a personal problem, not a collective responsibility. $310,000. That's the price tag we've put on the future. The question is, can we afford not to change it? What do you think needs to change? Drop your thoughts in the comments. Know someone struggling with this decision? Send them this. You're not alone in this impossible math.

- Raising Kids in America: The Hidden Struggles No One Talks About: Kind: captions Language: en So, you've made the big move to the United States. Now, you're thinking about raising a family here. Or maybe you already have kids and are wondering what's in store. You might have read plenty about American schools, playgrounds, and the land of opportunity for children. But what about the things no one really tells you, the stuff you only learn from living here day in and day out? Today, I'm sharing the real unfiltered truth about raising kids in America. What surprised me, what challenged me, and what I wish I'd known [Music] sooner. One, the pressure to do it all starts early. In America, the race seems to start before your child can even walk. There's a huge emphasis on early development. Mommy and Me classes, baby sign language, music lessons, baby yoga, you name it. Parents swap tips on the best preschools before their child's first birthday. It can feel overwhelming, especially if you come from a culture where childhood is a bit more relaxed. Two, school is a whole new world. American schools are a culture shock for many newcomers. From the pledge of allegiance to school spirit days, bake sales, and show and tell, there's a lot to take in. The school year often starts in August or September and ends in May or June with a long summer break. And don't be surprised if you're asked to volunteer, attend PTA meetings or send in snacks for the class party. Parental involvement is a big thing here. Three, the cost of child care and activities can be jaw-dropping. Child care in the US is expensive. Daycare for infants and toddlers can easily cost $1,000 to $2,000 a month per child depending on where you live. After school programs, summer camps, and extracurriculars like music, dance or sports can add hundreds more each month. Even public schools, which are tuitionfree, often ask for donations or fees for supplies, field trips, and activities. Four, safety is a constant conversation. Americans talk a lot about safety. There are rules and guidelines for everything. Car seats, bike helmets, food allergies, and even what kind of snacks you can pack for school. You'll get emergency drill notices from school, and stranger danger is a common topic. It's all meant to protect kids, but it can feel like a lot to keep up with. Five. Independence is encouraged, but not always how you'd expect. You might assume American kids are super independent, but there's a paradox. Parents are expected to supervise closely, yet kids are also encouraged to speak up, make choices, and pursue their interests. For example, play dates are scheduled in advance. Spontaneous play is less common than in many European countries. Sleepovers, sports teams, and summer camps are big milestones. Six. Food culture. Surprises and challenges. School lunches are a whole adventure. Pizza Fridays, peanut butter bands, and debates over healthy snacks are real. You might be surprised by what counts as a meal in the cafeteria. If you want your child to eat a certain way, packing lunches is common. But be ready for your kid to ask for whatever their friends are eating. Seven, diversity and identity. America is incredibly diverse and your child may meet classmates from all over the world. This can be a huge positive. Kids learn about different cultures, holidays, and traditions. But it can also come with questions about identity, especially if your family's background is different from the majority. Be ready to answer questions and help your child navigate their own sense of self. Eight. The health care maze. Pediatricians, specialists, dental checkups, and vaccines. Health care for kids in America is thorough, but it can be confusing and expensive. Insurance may not cover everything, and you'll need to keep track of forms, appointments, and vaccination schedules. Urgent care clinics are common for after hours needs, but always check what your insurance covers. Nine, the role of technology. Kids in the US grow up with screens everywhere. Tablets in preschool, Chromebooks in elementary school, smartphones by middle school. Schools often use apps to communicate with parents. Managing screen time is a constant challenge, and you'll need to set your own family rules. 10. Community is what you make it. This might be the most important thing. Community isn't automatic. You'll need to put yourself out there. Join parent groups, attend school events, chat with other parents at the playground. It can feel lonely at first, but with time you'll build your own support system. Many American parents are eager to connect, but you might have to make the first move. My biggest surprises and lessons. Birthday parties are a big deal. Expect themed parties, party favors, and sometimes even gift registries. School fundraising never stops. You'll be asked to buy everything from cookie dough to wrapping paper. Sports are huge. Some families spend every weekend at games or tournaments. There's an app for everything. School calendars, lunch menus, even bus tracking. Tips for newcomers raising kids in the US. Ask questions. Don't be afraid to check with teachers, other parents, or your pediatrician if you're confused. Budget for extras. Child care, activities, and optional school fees can add up fast. Keep your cultural traditions alive. Share your language, food, and holidays with your kids and their friends. Find your tribe. Look for expert groups, cultural organizations, or faith communities for support. Embrace the adventure. There will be challenges, but there are also amazing opportunities for your kids to grow, learn, and thrive. Final thoughts. Raising kids in America is a wild ride, full of surprises, challenges, and rewards. There's no one right way to do it, and every family's journey is unique. If you're open, proactive, and willing to learn, you'll find your way. And who knows, your kids might just teach you as much as you teach them. If you have questions, stories, or tips about raising kids in the US, share them in the comments, and don't forget to subscribe for more real life advice and stories about thriving in America. Thanks for listening, and I'll see you in the next one. Thanks for watching. We hope you found this video helpful as you prepare for your move to the United States. If you found value in the information and advice provided, please consider supporting the podcast on Patreon. Your donations will allow us to continue creating new episodes and videos to help even more people with their relocation to America. Just visit patreon.com/movingtotheus to sign up. Until next time, best of luck with your move.

- How to File Taxes in Two States (Easy Method): Kind: captions Language: en Welcome to Not Milk Never Milk. Subscribe if you're lactose intolerant. In this video, I will explain how to file taxes in two states using an easy method. Many people live in one state but work in another, making tax season a bit more complex. We will cover understanding your residency, identifying your doicile, and navigating non-resident filing. Our goal is to help you avoid double taxation and ensure a smooth process, saving you time and potential headaches. Let's simplify this often confusing topic together. First, it is crucial to understand your residency status. Your doicile is your true home, the place you always intend to return to, even if you are temporarily away. A non-resident, on the other hand, earns income in a state where they do not permanently reside. Some states have specific rules like considering you a resident if you spend more than 183 days within their borders. Knowing these definitions is your first step. Once you have clarified your residency, the filing process becomes clearer. You should generally file your non-resident state tax return first. This return reports only the income earned within that specific state. After that, you will file your resident state tax return, reporting all of your income, regardless of where it was earned. Crucially, you will then claim a tax credit on your resident return for the taxes you paid to the non-resident state, preventing double taxation. Finally, be aware of reciprocal agreements between states. These special agreements mean that if you live in one state and work in another with a reciprocal agreement, you only pay income tax to your resident state on your wages. This simplifies things considerably. Always maintain thorough records of your income, expenses, and any taxes withheld for both states. This diligent record keeping will be invaluable for accurate and stress-free filing. >> Thanks for watching. If this was helpful, leave a like and consider subscribing. See you next time.

- Filing Multiple State Income Tax Returns: Kind: captions Language: en hi I'm Jason for etx there are several reasons that someone may have to file more than one state tax return if you moved from one state to another mid year transferred to work in a different state for part of the year or you or your spouse worked in different states and you're filing jointly you may need to file partye or non-resident returns in multiple States if you've earned income in more than one state on your resident State return you must include all of your income for the year even that was earned out of state and then you must also file a non-resident return in your work state that means at times those working in multiple States may have a double tax burden on their outof state income for the year if this is the case you may be allowed a tax credit for the duplicate income taxes on your home state return if you moved between states this year and your move was permanent a part-year state return is required for each state that you lived in in this case you only have to include the amount of income earned in each state on their respective returns if you and your spouse worked in different states but you want to file your state tax returns jointly you may do so by filing jointly under both spouses names in both States but including only the income earned in each state by the one spouse for more information visit etax.com

  16. @zetto814: This is not about dual passports its about Israel being very sensitive to security at borders.

I have 2 passports and travel often. Never such a problem. I do things in the most logical order and in case of issue I show my other passport.

One thing people don't think about when you travel to the country of residence after a vacation using the other passport is the necessity to show that passport of your country of residence:

Let's say Rafael is now in Italy and wants to spend 2 weeks of vacation in Canada and enters Canada with his US  passport to avoid ETA fees and hassle. When he wants to get back to Italy he obviously has to use his US passport leaving Canada, however, Canadian border police might ask him a return ticket from Italy as US citizens aren't allowed to stay in Italy more than 90 days as visitors in Italy. In this case he informs them that he is an Italian citizen and shows them his Italian passport. Not all border police agents are as diligent, but happens to me half of the times when I get back to my country of residence after vacation/business trip.

Avoid presenting both passports simultaneously, present one and then the other only when they ask you about it.

X registered the ticket with the Canadian passport as he was currently in Canada.  

X enters YYZ and meets with American pre-screening CBP since he is entering the US through SFO to transit. 

X is legally bound to present a US passport as he is a US citizen re-entering his country.

CBP also checks the ticket and finds a mismatch between ticket (registered with his Canadian passport) 

CBP insists that when entering the country the passport to be used should be US.

The CBSA sees there is a mismatch for presenting a Canadian passport when the ticket is registered under US passport.

  4. @thejamericanleo [1 likes]: TLDW; follow the rules of the country you are leaving and heading to. For example, if you’re still a U.S citizen 

“U.S. nationals, including U.S. dual nationals, must use a U.S. passport to enter and leave the United States. U.S. dual nationals may also be required by the country of their foreign nationality to use that country’s passport to enter and leave that country. Use of the foreign passport to travel to or from a country other than the United States is not inconsistent with U.S. law. “

As for traveling to other countries outside of your nationalities, it really depends on which one gives you the best advantage for where you want to go. For example, I want to visit Ghana but on a U.S passport that would require a visa. However, on a Jamaican passport, I would be able to travel there visa free, and that’s the case for some other countries (Russia, Bangladesh, Zimbabwe, Tanzania, Kenya, etc.) where using that passport would be more suitable. 

Just be smart, but follow the laws and procedures.

Similarly, the US requires EU citizens to complete an ESTA visa waiver application for entry into the US. As I understand it, airlines are required to check to see if you have the ETIAS or ESTA visa waivers before allowing you to board.

So based on that, if I were to fly from my home in the US to visit family in Germany having dual passports, would this be the proper sequence of steps for traveling after ETIAS implementation:

Flying US to Germany:

1. Book roundtrip ticket using my German passport number.
2. At airline check-in desk, present my German passport. (If I presented my US passport without ETIAS, I expect I would be denied boarding).
3. I'm not sure which passport to present at TSA security checkpoints. I would be inclined to present my US passport because part of me thinks that, if I present my German passport, that could raise some red flags in their system because it may show that I never entered the country on my German passport. 
4. When boarding the aircraft, present my German passport and boarding pass. (No ETIAS needed for EU citizens.)
5. On arrival in Germany, present my German passport to border control.

Flying from Germany to US:

1. Present US passport at the airline check-in desk. (If I presented my German passport without an ESTA authorizing entry into the US, I expect I would be denied boarding.)
2. Present German passport at security and border control.
3. When boarding the aircraft, present my US passport and boarding pass. (No ESTA needed for US citizens.)
4. On arrival in the US, present my US passport to border control.

Does that make sense? Did I get anything wrong?

- Traveling | Basketball: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] in this video you will learn how not to travel traveling happens when you move with the ball without dribbling it is a violation which is penalized with a turnover to the other team to make sure you don't travel be careful in these three situations when you receive the ball while moving when you receive the ball while stationary and when you start dribbling first situation when you receive the ball while moving when you receive the ball if you take more than two steps without dribbling you will be called out for traveling to avoid this remember to take no more than two steps before releasing the ball for a shot or pass if you already have one foot on the floor when receiving the ball you only get one extra step second situation when you receive the ball while stationary to orientate yourself and protect the ball you can turn on one foot on the condition that this foot doesn't move this is called the pivot foot once you catch the ball if you take your pivot foot off the floor or slide it the referee will call it travel you may lift your pivot foot to shoot or pass but you must release the ball before your foot touches the ground again for information on how to choose your pivot foot check our video starts and stops third situation starting a dribble when you start dribbling if if you lift your pivot foot before you dribble a travel will be called to avoid this make sure you release the ball before moving your pivot foot the traveling rule can be tricky to get used to initially but it will quickly become natural and you won't even have to think about it over to you

- Study Guide: Trinidad and Tobago’s Dual Transition: Kind: captions Language: en Trinidad and Tobago is facing a modern problem. Its citizens are living longer, but the cost of keeping them healthy is spiraling out of control. Over 80% of deaths are now caused by non-communicable diseases or NCDs, chronic conditions like diabetes, cancer, and heart disease. A major driver is physical inactivity with a stark gender divide. Nearly 35% of women fail to get minimum weekly exercise. The medical consequences of these habits are piling up fast. Right now, nearly 40% of the adult population carries three or more risk factors for these chronic diseases. Treating multi-morbid patients requires extremely expensive lifelong interventions like dialysis or surgical amputations. These out-of-pocket costs are rapidly draining the fixed-income pensions that retirees used to rely on. Older citizens are backed into a corner. With failing health and depleted savings, they have to find aggressive new ways to generate cash just to survive. The most immediate solution for many older homeowners is looking right under their own roofs, converting downstairs annexes or spare bedrooms into independent short-term rentals. This graphic compares the capital needed for new builds versus renovations. Constructing a modest new home costs over 1 million Trinidad and Tobago dollars, entirely excluding the cost of land. So, retirees are logically choosing to adapt the property they already own. But, subdividing a house introduces severe bureaucratic friction. Landlords have to secure separate utility connections, meaning formal applications, inspections, and deposits with T&TEC, the National Electricity Commission, and WASA, the Water Authority. Then, there are the physical construction hurdles. Creating a commercially viable unit requires heavy interior work with basic materials like durable tile and secure partitioning running up high per square foot costs. On top of that, making a rental legal requires a formal change of use permit from the Town and Country Planning Division or TCPD. These spatial regulations often force landlords to physically alter their property boundaries to fit mandatory parking spaces and wheelchair ramps. What starts as a simple plan for side income quickly becomes a grueling physical and financial gauntlet heavily ill-suited for an elderly demographic already dealing with chronic illness. At the exact same time, the government is looking at this booming undocumented shadow rental economy and stepping in to formalize it. This flowchart breaks down the new 2026 landlord business surcharge. It forces all landlords to pay a $2,500 registration fee, followed by a tax of either 2.5 or 3.5% on their gross rental income. The state is treating evasion seriously. Missing an audit termers massive financial penalties, and any unpaid balances are hit with a heavy 15% annual interest rate, even if the rent was collected in foreign currency through platforms like Airbnb. Macroeconomic analysts point out an obvious ripple effect. Landlords operating on thin margins will simply pass this new tax burden directly onto their tenants, making affordable housing even harder to find. For the aging homeowner, the grassroots conversion route becomes a trap. Their private asset is transformed into a highly regulated, constantly taxed commercial entity eating away at the exact profit margins they need to pay for their health care. Recognizing this pressure, the government is opening an alternative route for wealth generation, the 2026 state-sponsored real estate investment trust or REIT. This diagram shows how the state plans to pool high-value, underutilized government office buildings and commercial land into a single, professionally managed trust. Instead of buying physical land, ordinary citizens can purchase fractional shares of this trust on the national stock exchange. This offers a sharp contrast to the active hustle of landlording. Investors earn regular, inflation-resistant dividends without negotiating over parking spaces, applying for water permits, or managing difficult tenants. It solves a major problem for the state. By monetizing idle properties through public equity markets, the government raises vital capital for the national budget without taking on expensive international debt. For an aging population, the REIT acts as a necessary escape hatch. It provides a highly liquid, low-friction vehicle to secure retirement income without picking up a hammer or navigating endless red tape. Residential properties now function as liquid financial engines optimized to provide the monthly cash flow that traditional pensions can no longer guarantee. Linking this financial evolution back to the medical crisis reveals a hard truth. Institutionalizing this wealth is the only way citizens can afford the proactive lifestyle changes they need like participating in the TT Moves campaign to combat the obesity and inactivity shortening their lives. Ultimately, surviving the decades ahead requires this dual transition. The state and its citizens must extract dormant wealth from a newly formalized real estate market to fund the epidemiological defense of a vulnerable nation.

And so um for the purposes of today's cons conversation, dual enrollment is an umbrella term used to refer to college courses offered to high school students regardless of instructor type, course location or modality. Um dual credit, you might have heard concurrent enrollment or dual credit. Um dual credit is used to pro refer to post-secary credits earned upon successful completion of dual enrollment coursework. So when we think about what are the benefits of dual enrollment to students, they are numerous. So one, high school learners receive early exposure to college rigor and expectations which can spark students post-secary aspirations. Um, secondly, students can explore potential careers and programs of study before fully committing to an institution or major, potentially saving thousands of dollars in tuition. Now, this is one piece of information that I wish I had known during my college career because there were times when I had to change my major, but and still had to be responsible for funding my college tuition. But just the thought of having the opportunity to explore these different pathways um before fully committing is a huge win um for learners. Numerous academic and state research studies confirm that students who have completed college coursework through dual enrollment are more likely one to metriculate in college, achieve post-secary success and not only start their degree but complete their degree on time or of ahead of time. Um, dual enrollment can also offer a pathway directly into the workforce for career and technical education students who earn an industry recognized credential before high school graduation, which is a huge win. All right, next slide, please. So, there are also um additional benefits um to students and their families. Um, one, we just mentioned it, which is that they can we it can reduce the cost of their certificate or their degree. Um, there are also benefits for high schools, which means that they are able to facilitate um have a more advanced facilitation offering of more advanced course options. Uh, high school teachers can better understand what the current uh, college culture is like because that may have changed drastically from the time that they were once in college. And then we cannot forget our advisement team. Um being able to have the additional support of advisors and counselors to better uh assist parents as they are exploring their college and career journey. Next slide. And the benefits extend also to post-secary institutions. Um it helps to build uh communication between the two from the high school to the college. there is uh increase of shared knowledge and best practices and it also offers opportunity for early marketing and student recruitment. So when we look at the high school textbook versus the post-secary textbook um there are a couple of differences here and one is that the textbooks that are used for post-secary courses are um typically more expensive. Um they are required to use the same or similar textbooks as equivalent to onampus offerings in the dual enrollment course. Um, we also have regional accredititors, state policies in many states and the national alliance of concurrent enrollment partnerships to uphold this standard. And most of us are aware that STEM textbooks are typically significantly more expenses than textbooks in the non- STEM disciplines. This is particularly helpful in underresourced school districts or financially challenged post-secondary institutions or among lowincome students. So if the dual dual enrollment textbook costs are born by districts, institutions or students and their families, use of OEER may eliminate this barrier. All right, next slide please. So the question we have all been waiting for. Who pays for dual enrollment textbooks? In 2021, MEC and SB completed a report to identify the responsible entity for covering dual enrollment textbook costs and followed up with another 50 state policy analysis in 2024, which we will share at the conclusion of this webinar. Since 2021, there has been very little change. Our 2024 report still shows that in majority of states, local secondary schools and post-secary partners determine who pays for the cost of dual enrollment textbooks or the policy is silent in that state. states that fund students dual enrollment tuition do not necessarily cover students dual enrollment textbook costs. Now, this was a huge aha moment for me. Um, you might have thought that the tuition would automatically cover the resources, but this is so pivotal, especially for um our underrepresented population. This makes a huge difference um to them having access to this opportunity. And so you will notice that 10 states um in 10 states the student or family is responsible for textbook costs. Um in 22 states we find that the policy is local determined or it is silent. And for about 17 states there are special programs offered to lowincome students for textbook courses with some conditions. So to view our state policy table, we will share a link and QR code at the end of this presentation to take you directly to that table beginning on page four. So while nearly all states have dual enrollment policies, we have some states with dual enrollment policies, other states with open education um policies, but there still seems to be like a missing link. like there still needs to be some intentionality behind the conversation of pairing the two. And so um before we move on um I just want to take this time to thank you for your attention and I am going to pass the mic to Anukica who will share insights about dual enrollment from the viewpoint of educators, administrators and policy leaders throughout our region. Thank you. >> Thanks Alison. Um yeah, so up until this point we've focused primarily on the opportunity that there are strong there's a strong alignment between the goals of dual enrollment and the goals of OAR. Um and we also know that there are gaps that still exist but and trying to connect these efforts in an intentional way. Um so what we did um to better understand what's happening on the ground is um we spoke to stakeholders across K12 higher ed and our state systems um to understand a little bit about um what what it actually takes in practice. So you know we can do what we can at the policy level and at the state level to try to connect these things. Um but we want to know what stakeholders are actually experiencing on the ground both the barriers um and the conditions that support success um in adopting OAR for dual enrollment context. So um I'm going to start with um some of the reflections that our stakeholders um told us um and we're going to cover some of the challenges, some of the bright spots and then the recommendations that emerge from their experiences. Um and this paper is also up on our website um so you can read more detail about what they had to say. So one of the clearest themes were um that um the challenges of putting OAR and dual enrollment together is not really about a lack of interest. Um there's there's a huge amount of interest. Um it's more structural. So stakeholders have repeatedly pointed to the disconnect between K12 and higher ed systems um which can comes create confusion around expectations, alignment, communication and ownership. They have also emphasized that faculty especially high school instructors teaching in the dual enrollment courses often don't have either the time or the support or the professional development needed to really um continue to identify, adapt and implement OAR effectively. And beyond that, there are practical challenges. Um there's an uneven availability of quality materials across disciplines. Um the the number of resources that instructors might need to sort through to to choose appropriate OAR for these courses is can be a barrier. Um we do continue to see some concerns about quality and credibility. just um mainly folks just not understanding what the open licenses mean and that um you know they can be just as a rigorous process of creating openly licensed materials as there are of creating publisher materials, but folks don't always understand that or have that context. And then just straight up logistical barriers like um high school students not always having access to the learning management systems um or consistent internet access. Next slide, please. But um despite these challenges, our stakeholders all shared some really clear success patterns with us as well. Um first, policy and leadership really matter. Um in states where in systems where there were mandates or there was funding or somebody articulated a really clear priority to include OAR in the dual enrollment practice, um we almost always saw significant adoption follow. Secondly, um faculty are much more likely to engage when they're given really strong starting points. Um so many of the faculty who um were able to come into this um opportunity with curated course materials, templates, discussion guides, ancillary materials to support um the use of the of the openly licensed textbook were were much more likely to be successful than those that were sort of left on their own to like piece together materials or figure it out. And then the third thing that we really heard a lot was that collaboration is critical. Um many of the folks that we talked to um said that what one of the benefits of doing this work was to have those um disciplinary based networks and communities of practice where high school teachers and college teachers could talk about their expectations for students and within their discipline and what they were expected to know and be able to do and try to close some of the gaps that we know exist between um where students sort of leave off in high school and what the expectations are for them in college. And finally, what we the other great thing that we saw was that um educators are innovating all around the barriers. So um they've come up with lots of creative solutions including um offline access solutions, shared systems, new ways of talking, new ways of meeting um which shows us that while there may be challenges in doing this, many of them are solvable with the right support structures. Next slide. So we asked our stakeholders to give us sort of two levels of recommendations. So one um from like strategy like what should we be doing better from a strategic standpoint and the second will be um based on implementation. So strategically um the recommendations really focused on creating the conditions u for sustainable largescale adoption. Um so first in that is communication. So communication needs to ext expand beyond the faculty and teachers to also include students, families, policy makers, making sure everyone understands like why we're doing what we're doing and how it's in support of students. Secondly, institutions need to intentionally create those communities of practice um especially at the discipline um so that um faculty and teachers can really work together and have that collaboration and ask questions of each other um and sort of um revise and remix and do all the great things you can do with open based on actual experiences in the classroom. Third, incentives matter. Um we can't get away from that. um wherever we can we want to be giving faculty and teachers um time, recognition, compensation um to do this work so that um so that their workload is not a barrier. And then finally, policy um came up repeatedly as a as a major lever which we mentioned earlier both at the state and at the institution level. Having something in writing that says these are our expectations and we expect you to do this um can really create that momentum for adoption and sort of normalize or institutionalize the practice of using openly licensed materials in the classroom. And then next one and then we asked um as I mentioned earlier what what do you need on the ground to actually make this work? So what do teachers and faculty actually need um when they're implementing OAR in a dual enrollment classroom? And here the focus what we heard was really about reducing that friction for faculty. And I'm sure Tabitha will talk about this in a minute. Um but one of the biggest barriers we heard repeatedly was just getting started. So finding the right um uh materials, having clear on-ramps, um having like a a cohesive um set of of ancillaries that go along with the materials, just making it as easy as possible for teachers and faculty to pick up the course materials and run with them. Uh stakeholders also emphasize that like that need for those full course packages, not just content um and having access um to the right technologies. Um especially the learning management systems that came up repeatedly as a potential barrier where they couldn't get all the students into like the college systems all the time. Um so they weren't able to always consistently access their course materials. Um and then professional development came up a lot. Um some folks said um you know they really appreciated having sort of basic what is OAR kind of um professional development to understand what they were doing with these open licensed materials. Um others were sort of like okay we get it we understand it like now what we need is how do we like make it even better? How do we implement some of those open pedagogy strategies? How do we adapt these materials and remix them for our specific students in front of us? So they were looking for that more advanced support. So having sort of a range of professional development available would be really helpful. And then finally again alignment and coordination came up over and over again from the on the ground experience. Um aligning schedules, aligning systems, aligning that technology so that um those things just don't get in the way and they don't create that friction or that noise around implementation. And now um oh yeah so in summary um going back to Allison's earlier um linkage note about the broken link um we really feel like that that there's a great potential in deliberately linking OAR policy with dual enrollment policy and initiatives um and it really has the opportunity to sort of um enhance both um innovations if you will um in in service of student success. Now I'm going to turn it over to Tabitha who's going to talk um as a teacher, as a faculty member um who's actually using OAR in the dual enrollment classroom and who participated in those stakeholder interviews that I just summarized. >> Thank you, Anakah. Hi everyone. Um I want to give you a little bit of background about me and the institution that I work for. Um I think that'll be um kind of helpful to see how our program even started. So I've been in education for almost 21 years and in that time the landscape of higher education has drastically changed. Um and one of those biggest changes is OAR. Um I remember when OAR was brought to me and I was asked hey do you want to teach a class in OAR and I was like absolutely not. I need my book. And I was willing to hold on to that book um as hard as I could until an instructor said, "How many times do you reference that book when you're teaching in the classroom?" And I said, "Uh, maybe twice." And she said, "Yes." She was like, "So, you can save your students a lot of money if we go to OEER." And that changed my perspective. I will be honest and say, "Hey, they might have drugged me kicking and screaming a little bit at times." Um but overall it's been one of the best choices that as an instructor I have made. So with that a little bit about UA Kasat. We are recognized as the leader in OAR in the state of Arkansas. We were the first institution um higher ed institution to adopt OEER practices. We are a small community college. We do have four campuses in southwest Arkansas. Um, we have agreements with numerous area high schools in the counties that we serve. Overall, our student population is about 1,300 students and we have around 600 students in our dual and um, concurrently enrolled programs and that's from 12 area high schools that we serve. We are also the only Hispanic serving institution in the state. So, one of the things when we started talking about OEER and it was brought up was the fact that our students, the majority of our students are eligible for pale. Um, so what we would find is a lot of times our students might enroll at the last minute. Pale was not available or even that um with pale they still needed to be able to pay for those textbooks which were extremely expensive. Um so with that we started looking at the impactful way that OEER um can help reduce cost. So with that said an 11year-old program has saved our students over $4 million in textbook rentals and purchases. That's an amazing number. Um, I remember when we hit a million, even just 500,000 was big. Um, to know that we saved our students that much money. Our program has grown. Um, when we first started, as I mentioned a while ago, our department, I teach English, we took all of our courses at one time into OEER. Now, we're at 80% of our courses across our entire um, college curriculum. One of the things that we have done and a lot of people ask and this has already been mentioned today um is funding. How do you fund this? How do teachers find time? Um group work is essential. Um work with your co-workers, share ideas. That's a huge part of this. But um we know that funding is limited. Um we work to build our program with limited local and state funding. Our instructors did get a stopend when we first started building OEER to help us um as we spent extra time outside of our work hours to build. So, moving forward to working with high school teachers. When we first started, it took our high school teachers um that work for us a little bit of time before they were ready to jump into OEER. Our high school instructors have to be credentialed the same as any other college instructor. So they must have a master's and complete it in 18 hours. So our high school teachers that teach concurrently on campus have different options of what type of textbooks they may use. Some of them do use OEER textbooks and they have access to that in our LMS which is Blackboard. Others have classroom sets of books and with those classroom sets they tend to maybe be a little outdated. English doesn't get outdated as quickly as say science. Um, but we do have um our instructors have the ability to have the option with that. Like I said, some of them opt for a tape a paper textbook. Those that have opted to use our OEER materials though will be given access to a class in our LMS and then they work usually with another instructor. Um, a lot of times we drop our class into a blank shell for them. We work with them on using Blackboard if they're not familiar. We go over um as best as we can OEER. Some of it is just diving in. Some of it is very scary and where do I look and is this good material with that. We think that mentoring um concurrent instructors is important and we do meet with our instructors concurrent our concurrent instructors at least once a year and go over questions they have and usually OEER is one of those things. I work closely with two instructors um who both use OEER and they use pretty much the same thing that we teach on our college campuses and they both jumped into it with both feet and have not taken a look back. Um an advantage to OAR materials for concurrent and dual enrolled students is the same as our traditional students. On that first day of class, everybody's on the same playing field. Nobody is waiting for a book. We don't hear um later in the semester, I left my book at home or my book is in the car because everything is electronic. In the case of our high school students, because they're using OEER, they don't have to come to campus to pick up a book. If they do come to campus, it's a rental. Um and I will tell you at my college, the school pays for that, I believe, but don't hold me to that. Um but with the use of OEER that also makes um I believe it was Allison said um the cost more affordable and with that students can get more college credit faster. Another advantage that I have seen um in using these online textbooks, these programs, these materials, our students have everything at their fingertips and they're used to having everything at their fingertips. They can access any of this from their phone. U most of them have grown up in the world of Google Classroom. Um so they're not strangers to using devices for reading and studying. So that is a a plus. um compared to non-traditional students who maybe have been out of school for a few years, they may be looking for that paper textbook. Um the high school students again tend to be less resistant to not having a physical textbook for the reasons I mentioned. A little bit of a disadvantage though with OEER um is the lack of quality internet. So, our students have to have good Wi-Fi and access to good Wi-Fi to be able to use it. And I know in the last two years, there have been times when our public schools, our students are enrolled in study halls and those um that Wi-Fi goes down and students will miss a deadline and we we have to we accommodate. But in that instance um because we are a royal area and not everyone has the same internet quality we do um see that as a disadvantage. Um another disadvantage and this comes straight from some of my high schoolers is that when we're providing OAR materials we want to give them all the things just as if they were getting a textbook. we have to pay attention to how we lay out that information and present it to students because they can get information overload and they will skip long passages um skip super long videos. Um so we have to be aware as instructors on what we give our students and again that's across the board and not just for our dual and concurrently enrolled. Um, our OEER materials allow us to cater to students who maybe need a reading level adjustment. We can find sources that adapt. We can find sources that help with um increasing the font size. So, when we have PDFs of books, students can zoom in. It will increase whatever they see on their screen. Um, one of the drawbacks though, and we'll catch up, is that we do have to make sure our documents are ADA compliant. Um, and then just for a couple of things to leave you with, um, student tips, and this comes straight from them, is if they're using OEER materials, they really like doing word searches. like say if they're answering a question on homework, they can search for a word and they really like doing things like that because it's easier to than flipping through a book because it highlights every page they need to find. Um, but one also said a drawback in that same sentence would be to um you're juggling multiple pages. So, you may have multiple tabs open. Um, so one of the things that you have to do is really work with your students on giving them suggestions for how to utilize the materials. Such as if I was giving an open book quiz, I might tell them to have two tabs open. One's the quiz and one is the other information. So, but overall working with students in high school and um for dual and concurrent programs, it's important that we treat them like they are traditional college kids. It's important that we um expose them to what a college classroom is like, the rigor that is involved, but it's also um extremely important to give them these opportunities. So OEER is one of those things that 11 years ago we didn't know where it was going to take us at Casat and now um we can see the advantages our concurrent enrollment has grown with accessibility um in so many ways. - How to Maintain Your U.S. Residency While Living Abroad: Kind: captions Language: en Many retirees and expats choose to live abroad for a more affordable and comfortable lifestyle. But one common concern is how to maintain US residency while enjoying life in another country. Understanding the rules and requirements can help you avoid complications with taxes, voting rights, and access to US services. One of the most important aspects of maintaining US residency is understanding the difference between residency for tax purposes and legal residency for state benefits. The United States does not require you to live within its borders to remain a citizen, but your tax obligations remain the same no matter where you live. The IRS requires US citizens to file taxes annually regardless of their country of residence. However, tools like the foreign earned income exclusion, FEIE, and the foreign tax credit can help reduce or eliminate double taxation. Keeping a US mailing address, preferably in a state with no income tax, can be beneficial for financial and legal reasons. Some expats use a family member's address or a mail forwarding service to maintain an official US address. State residency is another consideration, especially if you plan to return to the United States at some point. Some states make it easier than others to maintain residency while living abroad. If you are unsure of your state's policies, check with the Department of Motor Vehicles and local tax offices. Maintaining a driver's license, a registered vehicle, and voting rights in your home state can help establish continued residency. In many states, registering to vote with an overseas address may change your status to a non-resident voter, which could impact your ties to that state for tax purposes. Health insurance is another crucial factor if you plan to return to the United States for medical care. Maintaining a US-based health insurance plan is important. If you need affordable travel medical insurance in different countries, there is a link in the description. Medicare does not cover health care outside the United States. So, retirees often need an international health insurance policy. If you are under Medicare, maintaining an address in the United States is required to keep your coverage active for when you [Music] return. Some expats choose to enroll in Medicare Part A, which is free for most, and delay part B until they return to the United States to avoid unnecessary premiums. Banking and financial access also require careful planning. Some US banks may flag or restrict accounts if they suspect you are living abroad permanently. Keeping a US bank account open and using a US address for banking correspondence can help you avoid issues. Some banks and brokerage firms do not allow expats to open or maintain investment accounts while residing overseas. So, it is essential to check with your financial institution before moving. Using a credit card linked to a US billing address and maintaining some financial transactions in the US can further support your residency status. Ultimately, maintaining US residency while living abroad requires careful planning and documentation. Keeping ties through taxes, state residency, voting, banking, and health insurance ensures that you remain connected to the US while enjoying your life overseas. Understanding these key factors will help you avoid legal and financial complications and provide peace of mind as you explore retirement in another country.
