- Theories About Family & Marriage: Crash Course Sociology #37: Kind: captions Language: en You’ve probably heard people say that blood is thicker than water. That’s supposed to mean that family relationships – blood – are stronger than all other relationships. Which I guess are the water? But what do you think? Is that true? Can friends be considered your family? Or do you only think of your parents or siblings as family members? And how strong are familial relationships? Do they come with obligations that you don’t have for other people? Let’s try to tell the blood from the water here, and figure out what makes a family a family. [Theme Music] You probably have your own idea of what makes a family, but here’s how sociologists define it. Families are groups of people who are related by genetics, marriage, or choice, and who share material, emotional, and economic resources. So, the family isn’t a formal organization, like a government or a church. Instead it’s a social institution. That’s because the members of a family are held together by the commonly shared goal of the well being and mutual support of its members – and they organize people and power based on positions of social status within the family, like mother or daughter. Family often goes hand in hand with kinship, a social bond based on common ancestry, marriage, or adoption. These are the relationships that are most commonly thought of as ‘family’ – parents and kids, spouses, siblings, aunts and uncles. And some of these relationships you’re born into – the biological children of parents, for example. But other family relationships are created through legal bonds, like marriage or adoption. And it’s also important to recognize that for many people, family is a matter of choice. Marriage is the most prominent example of this – choosing to get married is two people coming together to say to each other and the world that they choose to be a family. Another family-by-choice is when close friends are also considered family, a practice called fictive kin. These family friends are incorporated into the larger family, interacting daily, living together, having their kids grow up together, calling the friend Uncle Joey – wait, that’s just the plot of Full House. But fictive kin isn’t a fictional concept. These found families consist of people who have chosen to care for each other, share resources, and share their lives together. This can make a family bond as strong as any based in ancestry or law. Now, no matter what type it is, the family that you grow up in is known as your family of orientation, because it orients you to the world and teaches you how families work. And the family that you create on your own, as an adult, is known as a family of procreation. A common type of family of procreation is a nuclear family, a unit made up of two parents and biological or adopted children. Consider this a contrast to another two common types of families: single parent families and extended families. A single parent family is exactly what it sounds like – one parent raising children. Both nuclear and single parent families are also sometimes referred to as immediate family – parents and siblings. Extended family is everyone in your family who isn’t a parent or sibling – grandparents, uncles, cousins, great aunts, third cousins twice removed. In the United States, most people live with their immediate family, though they may live nearby or regularly visit members of their extended family. And in many cultures – including in the US – families most often form around marriages. Marriage is a legally recognized relationship, usually involving economic, social, emotional, and sexual bonds. I know, that’s not a very romantic way to describe marriage. But marriage isn’t always based on love and romance. The idea of marrying for love alone is actually fairly new. Sure, people have always fallen in love. But you didn’t need to be in love to get married. Instead, more practical concerns – like creating tighter bonds between families, or finding economic security – have historically been more common reasons. Cultural norms – that are sometimes enshrined into laws – also limit romantic love by marking some people as acceptable spouses, or not. Some norms promote endogamy, or marriage between people of same social category. Essentially, like marries like. For example, college-educated Americans are more likely to marry other college-educated Americans. And people are more likely to marry others of the same race – though that pattern is slowly changing. Interracial marriage is an example of exogamy, or marriage between people of different social categories. Another example of exogamy is ‘marrying up’, where a person of a low socioeconomic status marries someone of a higher socioeconomic status. Traditionally, women are more likely to use marriage to move up the socioeconomic ladder than men, partially due to broader patterns of gender inequality that limit other opportunities for improving their status. And forms of marriage can vary across time and across cultures. In modern-day high income countries, marriage is, by law, only between two people – a practice known as monogamy. Polygamy, or marriage to two or more spouses, is legally recognized in the majority of African countries and in some South Asian countries. Marriage practices are deeply tied to the economic and social structures of society. For example, the most common form of polygamy is polygyny or the marriage of one man to two or more women. But in societies with low male-to-female ratios, polyandry, or the marriage of one woman to two or more men, can also occur. In countries where polygamy isn’t legally recognized, the term bigamy is used to refer to going through the legal processes of marrying while still married to another person. Now, marriage is the main way that individuals move from their family of orientation into a family of procreation. And with marriage comes decisions about how to fit your new family into your existing, separate families. Let’s go to the Thought Bubble to look at how marriage practices have varied across societies. Say a man and a woman get married…where should they live? If we’re in a pre-industrial society, the couple will most likely move to live with or near the husband’s family, a practice known as patrilocality. In a study of 1,153 cultures, cultural anthropologist David Levinson estimated that about 70% have a patrilocal residence pattern. This was most common for societies with frequent warfare, where families wanted to keep sons around as a form of protection. But in some pre-industrial societies, our newly married couple will practice matrilocality, where they live with or near the wife’s family. Many Native American tribes, particularly those in the Southwestern United States, such as the Hopi and Zuni, have historically been matrilocal, with newly married couples settling into the clan of the wife. In these societies, women generally own the home and resources of the family. These tribes are also matrilineal, meaning that they trace descent through the mother’s family tree. Most societies, however, are patrilineal, meaning that they trace kinship through the father. This means that all inheritance – land, wealth, titles – passes from father to son. We see patterns like this even in relatively recent times. Until the mid-nineteenth century, all land inherited by women in the US was solely owned by her husband, encouraging inheritance patterns that preference sons. And it was only in the last decade that Britain changed its line of succession to allow the monarchy to be passed on to the firstborn child, regardless of sex. Thanks Thought Bubble! So that’s how residential choice and family inheritances worked in the past. But what about nowadays? Industrialization has resulted in huge job growth in city centers, which has stoked greater migration from hometowns to big cities. As such, many married couples have been more likely to be living apart from both sets of parents, a practice known as neolocality. While patrilineal traditions, like passing on the father’s last name to children, persist, most families in industrial societies trace descent through both men and women. This continues to be true in most high-income countries, and neolocality is most common among the highly educated and households where both spouses are working. Because cities are where most of the jobs are. But even if modern day Americans live far from their parents, it’s impossible to escape the impact that families play in molding us, both on an individual and a societal level. Family is yet another social institution that we can use the three schools of sociological thought to examine. Structural functionalists emphasize the role that families play in socializing children, so they can function in society, while of course also giving them emotional and material support. But the other role of family in a structural functionalist framework is the regulation of sexual activity. Which again, I know, sounds super romantic. What I mean is that our societal norms about who can marry whom generally tells us something about what we hold to be acceptable or taboo sexual behavior. For example, incest is taboo in most cultures. This norm exists partially because reproduction among close relatives increases the likelihood of genetic inbreeding. These taboos mean that people must find their partners outside their family, creating social ties across families within a community. In contrast, social conflict theory, particularly feminist theory, focuses on the ways in which traditional notions about family perpetuate social inequality. Some of these inequalities might be obvious, based on what we’ve talked about already – like patrilineal lines of descent that make it impossible for women to hold wealth or gain power. There’s also the negative side of family as a form of sexual regulation. We might think it’s a good thing that family status ties prevent incest, but historically, this sexual regulation has also meant that married women have been seen as the sexual and economic property of their husbands. I’ve mentioned already that in the 19th century, women’s inheritance and earnings legally belonged to their husband. But for a more recent example, look at how the law treats women’s control over their own body within marriage: marital rape was not made illegal in all states in the US until 1993. Social conflict theory also points out the ways in which marriage entrenches social advantages and disadvantages. Because most people marry those who are similar to themselves, inequalities across class and race lines are often enhanced and further entrenched by marriage. Oftentimes, endogamous marriage occurs because of social norms and preferences. But it also stems from laws that regulate who can marry whom. Anti-miscegenation laws, which outlawed interracial marriage, were not ruled unconstitutional until 1967, in the aptly named Supreme Court case Loving vs Virginia. Finally, through the lens of symbolic interactionist theories, we can see families in terms of the daily interactions of family life. Family statuses come with their own expectations of behavior. The stereotypes of mother as nurturer and father as breadwinner shape how people think about themselves, and others, as ‘good’ or ‘bad’ fathers and mothers. Social exchange theory argues that relationships are a form of exchange between people, attempts to gain benefits from their interaction while avoiding the costs. If the costs outweigh the benefits, the marriage ends. Of course, much of what we’ve discussed today has looked at marriage under a heteronormative lens, and many of these theories don’t hold up as well when definitions of marriage and family are broadened to cover the wide spectrum of family types that we see today. So next week, we’ll talk about what families look in the here and now, and how society shapes these family forms. But today, we learned about how sociology defines family and the different terms used to describe specific types of family. We discussed marriage in different societies, as well as marital residential patterns and patterns of descent. Finally, we ended with a discussion of three schools of thought on the societal role of marriage and family. Crash Course Sociology is filmed in the Dr. Cheryl C. Kinney Studio in Missoula, MT, and it’s made with the help of all of these nice people. Our animation team is Thought Cafe and Crash Course is made with Adobe Creative Cloud. If you'd like to keep Crash Course free for everyone, forever, you can support the series at Patreon, a crowdfunding platform that allows you to support the content you love. Thank you to all of our patrons for making Crash Course possible with their continued support.

- The Double Life of a Family Man: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] Ever wonder what really goes on behind closed doors? We often assume that what we see on the outside is the whole story. But the truth is, every home, every family, and every individual has layers that remain unseen. The reality is far more complex than we might imagine. Your neighbor seems normal, maybe even happy and carefree. But statistics reveal a hidden reality lurking just beneath the surface. The people we pass every day, the ones we greet with a smile, maybe carrying secrets we can't even begin to guess. 73% of people leading double lives, report intense, constant stress. That's nearly three out of four. Imagine the weight of living each day with the fear of being found out. The anxiety of keeping your true self hidden from those closest to you. This kind of stress doesn't just fade away, it lingers, affecting every aspect of life. 40% admit to hiding a major secret from loved ones. Lifealtering secrets, not just small fibs. These are the kinds of truths that could change relationships forever if they ever came to light. The burden of keeping such secrets can be overwhelming, and it's more common than most people realize. The pressure to keep these secrets is immense, like holding your breath and never exhaling. It's a constant tension, a feeling that at any moment everything could unravel. For many, this pressure becomes a daily companion, shaping their actions and decisions in ways they may not even recognize. More than half of these secret keepers battle persistent anxiety, always worried about being discovered. The fear of exposure can be paralyzing, making it difficult to relax, to trust, or to truly connect with others. It's a silent struggle that often goes unnoticed by those around them. This isn't a rare phenomenon. It's a hidden struggle happening everywhere. In crowded cities, quiet suburbs, and small towns alike, people are wrestling with secrets that shape their lives in profound ways. It's a universal experience cutting across age, background, and circumstance. These numbers aren't just statistics. They're real people living with invisible burdens. Each percentage point represents someone you know, someone you care about, or maybe even yourself. The weight of these secrets can be crushing, yet most carry on as if nothing is wrong. Beneath the surface of everyday life, complex stories are unfolding. At the dinner table, in the workplace, or during a casual conversation, there are hidden narratives that influence how people think, feel, and interact. These stories are often filled with fear, hope, regret, and longing. People are juggling different versions of themselves, and the cost is high. The effort to maintain separate identities can lead to exhaustion, confusion, and a sense of isolation. It's a balancing act that few can sustain without consequences. The price is paid not just by the secret keeper, but by everyone around them. Families, friends, and partners often sense that something is wrong, even if they don't know what it is. The ripple effects of secrecy can strain relationships and erode trust over time. As we'll see, the impact of these secrets is deeper than we think. The hidden struggles of those around us shape our communities in ways we rarely acknowledge. Understanding this reality is the first step toward empathy, connection, and perhaps healing. Let's meet Mark. On the surface, he's the kind of person everyone seems to know. A friendly face in the neighborhood, a familiar presence at every school event, and the guy who always has a smile and a wave for passers by. Mark is in his early 40s, married to his college sweetheart, and together they're raising two energetic kids in a comfortable house, nestled in the suburbs. Their home is the kind of place where laughter spills out the windows, and the yard is always full of life. He's the dad on the sidelines coaching youth soccer with enthusiasm, the parent who volunteers for every school fundraiser, and the host of legendary backyard barbecues that bring the whole block together. Mark's weekends are a blur of family activities, community events, and friendly gatherings. To everyone around him, Mark is the definition of reliability. A steady, trustworthy family man who always seems to have it all together. He's the neighbor who shovels your driveway without being asked, and the friend who never forgets a birthday or special occasion. Need a handmoving furniture? Mark's there. Need someone to watch your kids in a pinch? He's already volunteered. He's the glue that holds his little corner of the world together. the person everyone counts on. But if you look a little closer, you might notice something different. There's a weariness in his eyes that doesn't quite match his easy smile. His phone is always face down on the table. And sometimes in the middle of a conversation, he seems to drift away, lost in thought. These are the subtle cracks in Mark's carefully polished facade. The small signs that not everything is as perfect as it appears. the stress lines, the moments of distraction, the quiet size when he thinks no one is watching. Behind closed doors, Mark is carrying a burden heavier than the demands of work or the challenges of fatherhood. It's a weight that presses on him day and night. A secret struggle that he keeps hidden from even those closest to him. Outwardly, Mark is living the dream. A loving family, a stable job, a beautiful home. But beneath the surface, there's a tension, a sense that something isn't quite right. Even in the happiest moments, there's a flicker of sadness in his eyes, a hint of distance in his smile. Mark is one of the 40% of people who are hiding a major secret from those around them. The pressure of keeping up appearances is mounting and the stress is beginning to seep into every part of his life. He's leading two lives, one that everyone sees and another that he keeps locked away. The effort to maintain this double existence is exhausting and it's starting to take a visible toll on his well-being. Mark's story is a reminder that even the most ordinary, picture perfect lives can hide extraordinary secrets. The people we think we know best may be carrying hidden struggles we can't even imagine. The truth is rarely as simple as it seems. Sometimes the real story is hidden just beneath the surface, waiting to be discovered. So, what's Mark's secret? It's a question that lingers in the air. One that even those closest to him would never think to ask. Mark on the surface is the picture of stability, a devoted husband, a loving father, and a reliable employee. But beneath that calm exterior, there's a hidden world, a life that no one suspects. It's not espionage or crime. There's no cloak and dagger, no secret meetings in shadowy alleys. Mark's secret is far more ordinary yet just as consuming. A secret that's quietly shaped every decision he's made for years. Years ago, Mark was a passionate day trader, spending long nights glued to his computer, chasing the thrill of the market. He lived for the rush, the possibility of turning a small investment into something big. But then, a devastating loss wiped out his savings and shattered his confidence. The pain was too much to bear, and he promised himself and his wife Sarah that he was done for good. He promised Sarah he was done, got a stable job, and moved on, or so it seemed. He focused on his family, building a new life, one that was safe and predictable. For a while, he convinced himself that was enough. But three years ago, the old itch returned. Craving excitement, Mark secretly started trading again. He told himself it was just a hobby, a harmless way to blow off steam after work. But the thrill was addictive, and soon he was spending more and more time in front of the screen, chasing that elusive high. It began small, but soon he was managing a substantial hidden portfolio. The stakes grew higher and so did the risks. Mark found himself living a double life, carefully hiding every trade, every win and loss from his family. To keep his secret safe, he created a second identity. Marcus, his online trading persona. Under this alias, he joined forums, shared tips, and built a reputation in the digital world that was completely separate from his real life. At home, Mark was a dependable dad, always present for bedtime stories and weekend soccer games. But as Marcus, he was a risk-taker, living on the edge in a secret world of numbers and adrenaline. He even rented a small office, telling Sarah it was for work overflow. In reality, it was his sanctuary, a place where he could trade without fear of being discovered, where Marcus could exist without boundaries. There, surrounded by glowing monitors and the hum of computers, Mark felt powerful and alive. But the freedom came at a cost. Every moment spent as Marcus was a moment spent lying to his family. And the weight of that deception grew heavier with each passing day. Every win was a secret, every late night a lie. The victories felt hollow, tainted by the knowledge that he couldn't share them with the people he loved most. The thrill was always shadowed by guilt. Mark would catch his own reflection late at night and wonder how long he could keep up the charade. Mark was living as two men, separated by a wall of secrets. Each day, the line between Mark and Marcus blurred a little more, making it harder to know where one ended and the other began. Maintaining that wall was exhausting. The lies piled up, and the fear of being discovered haunted him. Even in his happiest moments, the double life was both his escape and his prison. A secret world that gave him purpose, but also threatened to take everything he loved away. Keeping two lives separate is a full-time job on top of his real one. Mark spent an extra 60 hours a week just managing the lie. Early mornings secret office trips, clearing browser history, juggling emails and bank accounts. He'd trade before dawn, multitask at work, and use lunch breaks for the market. Evenings meant more lies, late meetings, hours at his secret office. He kept a separate wardrobe, planned every detail, and covered every contingency. The mental energy to switch personas was immense. Mark had burner phones, encrypted apps, and alibis for his alibis. The web of lies was becoming unmanageable. One slip could bring it all crashing down. The effort was monumental and unsustainable. The house of cards was trembling. The most visible casualty of Mark's double life, his absence at home. He missed family dinners five nights a week, always with a new excuse. At first, Sarah was understanding, but the empty chair became a symbol of growing distance. His daughter stopped asking when he'd be home. His son waited up only to find Mark distracted or asleep. The daily connections that make a family were eroding. Sarah became a single parent in all but name, handling everything alone. Conversations with Mark turned brief and logistical. Emotional intimacy faded, replaced by polite distance. Mark saw the damage but justified it as a temporary sacrifice for a better future. In trying to provide, he was dismantling his family's foundation. The emotional cost was far greater than any financial gain. The family was paying the price for his secret. The stress of Mark's double life didn't stay in his mind. It attacked his body. He suffered constant headaches, popping painkillers for relief that never lasted. Sleep was rare. His mind raced with market worries and lies. He'd be awake at 3:00 a.m. checking trades, running on caffeine and adrenaline. Exhaustion and irritability became his norm. Meals were unhealthy, grabbed on the go, and he gained weight. Digestive issues and a general malaise set in. His body was keeping score, warning him to stop. Chronic stress like this leads to real health problems. High blood pressure, heart disease, a weakened immune system. The body can't tell the difference between a real threat and the stress of a secret life. Mark was accelerating his own decline. Mark's mind was under siege. anxiety, a constant companion. A simple question from Sarah could trigger panic. He rehearsed stories, anticipated discovery, and became hypervigilant. Every interaction was calculated, every word monitored. The emotional isolation was profound. He couldn't share his biggest wins or losses. The gap between him and Sarah grew wider. This mental state is unsustainable. Psychologists call it cognitive dissonance, living with contradictions that erode your sense of self. Mark rationalized his actions, but the mental gymnastics were exhausting. Over time, the anxiety threatened to consume him. Why do people like Mark think they can keep it up? Every successful lie reinforced his illusion of control. Early successes built false confidence. He thought he could outsmart everyone. The secret world felt safer and more predictable than messy family life. In trading, he was in charge. At home, life was unpredictable. The belief that a double life can last is denial, a defense against guilt and fear. People convince themselves they're the exception, that their system is perfect. But the emotional walls are dissolving and the impact seeps into everything. The illusion of control can only last so long. Eventually, reality catches up. Mark's story is a reminder. Secrets are rarely born from malice, but from human longing. He thought he was protecting his family, but the secret became a weapon. The real cost isn't just exposure or financial loss. It's the slow death of intimacy. Missed moments, lost connections, and broken trust are the true price. We aren't built for compartmentalization. We crave authenticity. The energy to maintain two selves drains our health and relationships. The fear and anxiety are signs we're living out of alignment. The truth always finds a way out. Sometimes slowly, sometimes all at once. When the house of cards falls, the damage is real. The lesson, the most valuable thing we can offer is our whole undivided self. That's the only foundation that truly lasts.
